meta.cardTitle: Exam 2 Review — Weeks 4-7: Vitamins → GI & Diabetes

questions.stem: A client comes to the clinic reporting difficulty seeing while driving at dusk and at night, though daytime vision is unchanged. The client follows a very low-fat diet and rarely eats orange or dark-green vegetables. Which deficiency is the nurse most likely to suspect?
opts: Vitamin A deficiency
Vitamin D deficiency
Vitamin E deficiency
Vitamin K deficiency
questions.rat: Vitamin A forms rhodopsin in the retina; night blindness is the first sign of deficiency, and a low-fat diet impairs absorption of all fat-soluble vitamins. Vitamin D deficiency causes bone disease (rickets/osteomalacia), vitamin E causes neuromuscular/hemolytic problems, and vitamin K causes bleeding — none present first as night blindness.
questions.strat: Match the earliest symptom to the vitamin's job: vision at night points straight to vitamin A.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is starting isotretinoin (Accutane) for severe acne. Which statement by the nurse is most important to include in teaching?
opts: "Use a reliable method of birth control during the entire course of this drug."
"Take this medication on an empty stomach each morning to improve absorption."
"Add servings of liver and carrots daily to support your skin while on this drug."
"Limit green leafy vegetables to prevent a serious drug-nutrient interaction."
questions.rat: Isotretinoin contains vitamin A, and excess vitamin A is teratogenic; women of childbearing age must use reliable contraception because use in pregnancy causes birth defects. Adding liver and carrots would raise vitamin A intake further and worsen the risk, and the green-vegetable caution applies to vitamin K and anticoagulants, not this drug.
questions.strat: Vitamin A in excess = birth defects; any vitamin-A-based drug in a fertile woman means contraception teaching first.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a community group about vitamin D. For each statement, indicate whether it is Accurate or Inaccurate.

rows.t: Vitamin D helps the body absorb and regulate calcium and phosphorus for bone mineralization.
The skin can make vitamin D when exposed to sunlight.
Older adults usually need less vitamin D because their skin makes it more efficiently with age.
A deficiency of vitamin D in children can lead to rickets.
Taking very high doses of vitamin D long term is harmless because any excess is simply excreted in urine.

questions.rat: Vitamin D regulates calcium and phosphorus for bone, is synthesized in skin with sun exposure, and its deficiency causes rickets in children. Older adults need MORE vitamin D because aging skin synthesizes less, and because it is fat-soluble and stored, excess causes toxicity (hypercalcemia) rather than being freely excreted.
questions.strat: Fat-soluble vitamins are stored, not flushed — 'extra just leaves the body' is almost always the false statement.

questions.stem: Match each fat-soluble vitamin to the deficiency finding most characteristic of it.
questions.rat: Vitamin A deficiency first shows as night blindness (rhodopsin); vitamin D deficiency causes rickets in children; vitamin K deficiency impairs clotting factor synthesis, causing bleeding; vitamin E deficiency causes anemia and neurological/muscular deficits. Each finding ties directly to that vitamin's primary physiologic role.
questions.strat: Anchor each vitamin to its one job — A=eyes, D=bone, K=clotting, E=antioxidant/cell membranes — then the deficiency follows.

questions.stem: A client newly prescribed warfarin asks the nurse about diet. The client typically eats large daily servings of kale, spinach, and broccoli. Which response by the nurse is correct?
opts: "Keep your daily intake of those green vegetables fairly steady."
"Drop the green vegetables from your diet while taking this medication."
"Eat extra green vegetables daily, since they boost the action of warfarin."
"Green vegetables do not affect warfarin, so vary the amount as you wish."
questions.rat: Green leafy vegetables are high in vitamin K, which decreases the effectiveness of warfarin; the goal is consistent, not zero, intake so the dose can be balanced against it. Eliminating them entirely is unnecessary and unrealistic, eating extra would reduce the drug's effect, and saying there is no effect is false.
questions.strat: Vitamin K opposes warfarin — teach consistency, not elimination; 'all' or 'never' options are usually wrong.

questions.stem: Parents of a healthy newborn ask why their baby is receiving a vitamin K injection shortly after birth. Which explanation by the nurse is most accurate?
opts: "A newborn's sterile gut cannot make vitamin K yet, so the shot helps prevent bleeding."
"The shot replaces the vitamin D your baby needs for bone growth in the first weeks."
"The shot strengthens your baby's immune system until breastfeeding is well established."
"The shot supplies the iron your baby lost during the stress of the birth process."
questions.rat: Newborns are born with a sterile gut and lack the intestinal bacteria that synthesize vitamin K, so they cannot make enough to support clotting and are at risk for bleeding; the injection provides it until gut flora develop. The other options confuse vitamin K with vitamin D, immune support, or iron, none of which is the reason for this injection.
questions.strat: Sterile newborn gut = no vitamin K = bleeding risk = the after-birth injection.

questions.stem: A client is taking high-dose over-the-counter supplements of vitamins A, D, E, and K. Which findings should the nurse recognize as possible signs of fat-soluble vitamin excess and report? Select all that apply.
opts: Vomiting and anorexia from excessive vitamin A
Hypercalcemia from excessive vitamin D
Cessation of menstrual periods from excessive vitamin A
Increased bleeding when vitamin E supplements are combined with an anticoagulant
Night blindness caused by too much vitamin A
Rickets caused by too much vitamin D
questions.rat: Because fat-soluble vitamins are stored, excess is harmful: vitamin A toxicity causes vomiting, anorexia, and menstrual cessation; vitamin D toxicity causes hypercalcemia; and high-dose vitamin E with an anticoagulant raises bleeding risk. Night blindness and rickets are signs of DEFICIENCY, not excess, so they are not reported as toxicity findings.
questions.strat: On a 'toxicity' SATA, screen out the deficiency look-alikes (night blindness, rickets) — those belong to the opposite list.

questions.stem: A nurse teaches a client about taking fat-soluble vitamin supplements. To absorb best, the client should take them [1]. The client is also taught that, unlike water-soluble vitamins, fat-soluble vitamins are [2], which is why exceeding the upper limit can be harmful.

blanks.label: [1] Timing for best absorption
opts: with a meal that contains some dietary fat
on an empty stomach first thing in the morning
with a large glass of water between meals

blanks.label: [2] Why excess is harmful
opts: stored in body fat rather than readily excreted
destroyed quickly by stomach acid
flushed out in urine within a few hours

questions.rat: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) depend on dietary fat for absorption, so taking them with a fat-containing meal improves uptake; because they are stored in fatty tissue rather than readily excreted, intake above the upper limit accumulates and can cause toxicity. The empty-stomach and water-flush options describe water-soluble vitamins.
questions.strat: Fat-soluble = take WITH fat and they STAY in the body — both blanks key on 'fat' and 'stored.'

questions.stem: Match each water-soluble vitamin to the deficiency disease classically associated with it.
questions.rat: Thiamine deficiency causes beriberi; niacin deficiency causes pellagra (the three D's: dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia); B12 deficiency from absent intrinsic factor causes pernicious anemia; inadequate folate during early pregnancy causes neural tube defects; vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy. Students often swap B12 and folate because both produce a megaloblastic anemia, but only B12 loss from intrinsic-factor failure is called pernicious anemia.
questions.strat: Map each vitamin to its signature disease name; B12 owns the word 'pernicious,' folate owns 'neural tube.'

questions.stem: A client admitted for alcohol withdrawal presents with confusion, an unsteady gait, and abnormal eye movements. The provider orders IV vitamins before any dextrose-containing fluids are started. Which vitamin deficiency do these findings most likely reflect?
opts: Riboflavin (B2)
Thiamine (B1)
Pyridoxine (B6)
Cobalamin (B12)
questions.rat: Confusion, ataxia, and ocular changes in a client with chronic alcohol use point to thiamine deficiency (Wernicke-Korsakoff, the cerebral form of beriberi); thiamine is given before dextrose to avoid worsening the neurologic injury. Riboflavin deficiency causes cheilosis and glossitis, B6 deficiency causes dermatitis and neuropathy, and B12 deficiency is more typically a slowly progressive neurologic and hematologic problem.
questions.strat: Alcohol + confusion + ataxia = thiamine; give it before glucose.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old who is not pregnant tells the clinic nurse she hopes to conceive within the next year. Which teaching about folic acid is most appropriate?
opts: Start a folic acid supplement now, before she conceives, to lower defect risk
Wait until her pregnancy test is positive, then begin folic acid for fetal growth
Folic acid matters once she develops anemia in the second or third trimester
A prenatal vitamin started in the third trimester will supply enough folic acid
questions.rat: Neural tube closure happens in the first weeks of pregnancy, often before pregnancy is recognized, so folic acid must be in place beforehand; supplementation is recommended for all women of childbearing age planning conception. Starting after a positive test, waiting for anemia, or relying on a third-trimester vitamin all miss the early window when the neural tube forms.
questions.strat: Neural tube closes before she knows she's pregnant; folate has to be on board first.

questions.stem: A nurse is identifying clients at increased risk for vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency. Select all clients who are at higher risk.
opts: An 80-year-old with reduced intrinsic factor from gastric mucosal changes
An adult who follows a strict vegan diet with no supplementation
A client recovering from a partial gastrectomy
A young adult who eats meat, eggs, and dairy daily
An adult who eats several servings of citrus fruit each day
questions.rat: B12 absorption depends on intrinsic factor and on intake from animal foods, so older adults with declining intrinsic factor, vegans (B12 is absent from plant foods), and clients with gastric surgery affecting intrinsic factor are all at risk. The client eating meat, eggs, and dairy has ample B12 intake, and citrus intake addresses vitamin C, not B12.
questions.strat: B12 needs intrinsic factor AND animal foods; lose either pathway and risk climbs.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching about water-soluble vitamins. Because these vitamins are [1] in the body, excess amounts are [2], which means a person must obtain them [3].

blanks.label: [1] storage
opts: readily stored in fat and the liver
not stored to any meaningful degree
converted to fat-soluble forms

blanks.label: [2] excess handling
opts: excreted in the urine
retained in adipose tissue
stored for months of reserve

blanks.label: [3] intake pattern
opts: only during illness
in a single large weekly dose
regularly through diet or supplements

questions.rat: Water-soluble vitamins are not stored in appreciable amounts; excess is excreted in the urine, so they must be consumed regularly. That contrasts with fat-soluble vitamins, which are stored in fat and the liver and can build to toxic levels. The distractors reflect the common error of applying fat-soluble storage behavior to the water-soluble group.
questions.strat: Not stored -> excreted -> needed regularly; that triad defines water-soluble vitamins.

questions.stem: A client taking an oral iron supplement for iron-deficiency anemia asks how to get the most benefit from it. Which suggestion by the nurse is based on vitamin C's role in nutrition?
opts: Take the iron with a glass of orange juice
Take the iron with a tall glass of milk to settle the stomach
Take the iron together with your calcium supplement
Take the iron right after your high-fiber bran cereal
questions.rat: Vitamin C enhances the absorption of non-heme (plant and supplement) iron, so pairing the iron with a vitamin C source such as orange juice increases uptake. Milk and calcium reduce iron absorption, and the phytates in high-fiber bran cereal also inhibit it.
questions.strat: Vitamin C boosts non-heme iron; pair iron with a citrus source, not dairy.

questions.stem: A nurse is reviewing the diet of an older adult with a slow-healing pressure injury, bleeding gums, and a history of eating mostly canned and shelf-stable foods with few fresh fruits and vegetables. Which nutrient deficiency best explains this picture?
opts: Niacin (B3)
Vitamin C
Riboflavin (B2)
Biotin (B7)
questions.rat: Vitamin C is required for collagen formation and wound healing; its deficiency (scurvy) causes impaired healing and bleeding gums, and a diet lacking fresh fruits and vegetables is a classic risk. Niacin deficiency causes pellagra (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia), riboflavin deficiency causes cheilosis and glossitis, and biotin deficiency causes a scaly rash and hair loss rather than this bleeding-gum, poor-healing pattern.
questions.strat: Collagen + bleeding gums + no fresh produce = vitamin C (scurvy).

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a client about food sources of B vitamins. Classify each statement as accurate or inaccurate.

rows.t: Vitamin B12 is found in meat, fish, eggs, and dairy but not in plant foods
Folate is found in leafy green vegetables, legumes, and enriched grains
Citrus fruits and other fresh fruits and vegetables are good sources of vitamin C
Vitamin C is found mainly in red meat and organ meats
Folate occurs only in animal products, so vegans cannot meet their needs

questions.rat: B12 comes from animal foods only, folate from leafy greens/legumes/enriched grains, and vitamin C from fruits and vegetables. The two inaccurate rows reverse the food groups: vitamin C is not a meat nutrient, and folate is abundant in plant foods, so a vegan can readily meet folate needs (B12, not folate, is the vegan concern).
questions.strat: B12 = animal foods; folate and vitamin C = plant foods. Don't swap them.

questions.stem: A client says, "I leave the milk in a clear glass pitcher on the counter so the kids can reach it." Knowing milk is a major source of riboflavin, which response by the nurse is most accurate?
opts: Riboflavin breaks down in light, so milk keeps best in an opaque jug
Leaving milk out actually raises its riboflavin as it warms on the counter
Riboflavin is not affected by light, so the clear glass pitcher changes nothing
Boiling the milk before you serve it will restore the riboflavin it has lost
questions.rat: Riboflavin is sensitive to light, which is why milk is sold in opaque containers; storing it in clear glass in the light causes riboflavin loss. Warmth does not add the vitamin, light does affect it, and heating cannot restore a vitamin already destroyed.
questions.strat: Riboflavin is light-sensitive; opaque container protects it.

questions.stem: A nurse counsels a client newly diagnosed with iron-deficiency anemia who follows a vegetarian diet and relies on lentils, spinach, and fortified cereal for iron. Which instruction best improves the client's iron absorption from these foods?
opts: Pair the lentils and spinach with citrus fruit or peppers
Drink black tea or coffee with each of the iron-rich meals
Take a calcium supplement together with the fortified cereal
Eat the spinach raw rather than lightly steaming it first
questions.rat: Non-heme iron from plants is absorbed far less efficiently than heme iron; vitamin C (citrus, peppers) converts iron to a more absorbable form and enhances uptake. Tea (tannins) and calcium both inhibit non-heme iron absorption, and cooking does not destroy the mineral, so eating it raw confers no absorption benefit.
questions.strat: Vitamin C boosts non-heme iron; tea and calcium block it.

questions.stem: A nurse is identifying clients in a community clinic who are at highest risk for iron-deficiency anemia. Which client should the nurse prioritize for iron-status screening and dietary teaching?
opts: A pregnant woman in a low-income household
A man who eats red meat several times a week
An adult who takes a daily vitamin C supplement
A teenager who drinks fortified milk daily
questions.rat: Per the WHO/CDC data in the source, iron deficiency is the most common micronutrient malnutrition worldwide, and pregnant women in low-income households are a top at-risk group; pregnancy raises iron demand and 1 in 6 women is iron-deficient in pregnancy. Frequent red-meat intake and vitamin C both support iron status, and fortified milk is not a key iron source.
questions.strat: Match the at-risk group to increased demand plus limited intake.

questions.stem: A nurse reviews the diet of a client who avoids all dairy and reports muscle twitching and occasional spasms in the hands. Which dietary pattern most likely explains these findings?
opts: Inadequate calcium intake from dairy alternatives
Excessive sodium intake from heavily processed foods
Inadequate potassium intake from fruits and vegetables
Excessive phosphorus intake from dark cola beverages
questions.rat: Calcium supports nerve function and muscle relaxation/contraction; low intake can produce neuromuscular irritability such as twitching and tetany. A dairy-avoidant client who replaces nothing with fortified soy, canned fish with bones, tofu, or greens is at risk. Sodium and phosphorus excess and potassium deficiency cause different problems and do not best fit the dairy-avoidance picture.
questions.strat: No dairy plus twitching/spasm points to calcium and tetany.

questions.stem: A nurse teaches an older adult about bone health. Calcium is important for healthy bones and teeth, and its use by the body depends on adequate [1]. A client who avoids dairy can still meet calcium needs by choosing [2].

blanks.label: [1] Needed for calcium use
opts: vitamin C
vitamin D
vitamin K

blanks.label: [2] Non-dairy calcium source
opts: white rice and pasta
fortified soy milk and canned fish with bones
lean chicken breast

questions.rat: Calcium requires vitamin D for absorption and use, a key teaching point for bone health. Non-dairy calcium sources from the source list include fortified soy milk/tofu, canned fish with bones (salmon, sardines), and greens; refined grains and chicken are not meaningful calcium sources.
questions.strat: Calcium needs vitamin D; canned fish-with-bones and fortified soy are non-dairy options.

questions.stem: A nurse works in a region where some households use only non-iodized specialty salt and eat little seafood. A client asks how to prevent the goiter that affected an older relative. Which response by the nurse is most accurate?
opts: Iodine is needed to make thyroid hormone; use iodized salt or eat seafood
Iodine builds strong bones and teeth; increase the leafy greens in your diet
Iodine carries oxygen through the blood; eat more red meat and dried fruit
Iodine helps regulate blood glucose; choose whole grains and brewer's yeast
questions.rat: Iodine is a component of thyroid hormone, which regulates growth, development, and metabolism; deficiency causes goiter. Iodized salt, seafood, and dairy supply iodine. The other options misattribute the roles of calcium/fluoride (bones), iron (oxygen transport), and chromium (glucose regulation).
questions.strat: Iodine to thyroid to goiter; iodized salt prevents it.

questions.stem: Match each trace mineral to its primary physiologic function.
shared: Carries oxygen as part of hemoglobin in red blood cells
Component of thyroid hormone regulating metabolism
Supports wound healing, immune function, and taste perception
Works with insulin to regulate blood glucose
questions.rat: Iron is part of hemoglobin and carries oxygen; iodine is part of thyroid hormone; zinc supports wound healing, immune health, and taste perception; chromium works closely with insulin to regulate glucose. These are the source-defined functions for each trace mineral.
questions.strat: Iron-oxygen, iodine-thyroid, zinc-healing, chromium-glucose.

questions.stem: A nurse teaches a client about the electrolyte minerals sodium and potassium and how to obtain them from food. Which statements should the nurse include? Select all that apply.
opts: Sodium is the major extracellular electrolyte and helps regulate fluid balance
Potassium is found in fresh fruits, vegetables, meats, and whole grains
Most dietary sodium comes from processed foods and table salt
Both sodium and potassium support nerve transmission and muscle contraction
Potassium is the major extracellular electrolyte for fluid regulation
Sodium provides the body with a direct source of energy
questions.rat: Sodium is the major extracellular electrolyte for fluid and acid-base balance, supplied largely by processed foods and table salt; potassium is the major intracellular electrolyte found in fruits, vegetables, meats, and whole grains; both support nerve transmission and muscle contraction. Potassium is intracellular (not extracellular), and minerals do not provide energy once absorbed.
questions.strat: Sodium = extracellular, potassium = intracellular; minerals are catalysts, not fuel.

questions.stem: A public health nurse explains why staple foods like flour, rice, and cereal are fortified in a community where many families can afford only cheap, filling foods. Which statement best describes the goal of this fortification?
opts: To add vitamins and minerals that low-micronutrient diets lack
To raise the calorie content of inexpensive staple foods
To extend the shelf life of grains during long-term storage
To replace the need for fresh fruits and vegetables in the diet
questions.rat: Fortification adds essential vitamins and minerals (e.g., iron, folic acid, B12 to flour and rice) to help populations meet intake and prevent the 'hidden hunger' of micronutrient deficiency in diets of cheap, filling foods. It is not aimed at adding calories or shelf life, and it supplements rather than replaces a varied diet.
questions.strat: Fortification targets micronutrient gaps, not calories or shelf life.

questions.stem: A client recovering from a major burn injury asks the nurse why the dietitian keeps emphasizing fruits and vegetables. The nurse explains that tissue trauma and inflammation increase the client's exposure to free radicals. How should the nurse describe what antioxidants do in this situation?
opts: They neutralize free radicals and limit the cell damage of oxidative stress
They bind dietary fat so that fewer free radicals are absorbed from the food eaten
They raise the number of free radicals the cells produce during the healing process
They replace the calories the body burns through while fighting off the infection
questions.rat: Antioxidants counteract free radicals and protect cells from the damage (oxidative stress) that leads to apoptosis or necrosis; tissue trauma and inflammation are listed sources of free-radical production. They do not bind fat for absorption, do not increase free-radical output, and are not a calorie source.
questions.strat: Map the term to its mechanism: antioxidant = anti-(free radical) = less oxidative stress.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a community group about eating a diet rich in antioxidant vitamins and minerals. Which foods should the nurse include as good sources of antioxidants? Select all that apply.
opts: Citrus fruits and strawberries
Carrots and sweet potatoes
Nuts and plant-based vegetable oils
Fish, eggs, and mushrooms
White bread and refined pasta
questions.rat: Citrus and strawberries supply vitamin C; carrots and sweet potatoes supply vitamin A; nuts and vegetable oils supply vitamin E; fish, eggs, and mushrooms supply selenium. Refined, processed foods are listed as a source of oxidative stress, not a source of antioxidants.
questions.strat: Color and whole-food variety signal antioxidants; refined/processed signals the opposite.

questions.stem: A healthy adult with a varied diet tells the nurse, "I just buy antioxidant pills instead of bothering with produce." Which response by the nurse best reflects current evidence on antioxidant intake?
opts: "Getting your antioxidants from a varied diet of fruits and vegetables is the preferred choice."
"Supplements are equivalent to food, so either choice gives you the same protection from disease."
"Supplements actually work better than food because the dose packed into each pill is far higher."
"You can safely cut back on antioxidant-rich produce once you begin taking a daily supplement."
questions.rat: The preference is meeting nutrient recommendations through a healthy, nutrient-rich diet; research has not shown that supplementation supports disease prevention. Supplements are not proven equivalent or superior, and produce should not be reduced. Supplementation is reserved for malabsorption or disease states that impair absorption.
questions.strat: Default to food first; supplements are the exception (malabsorption), not the rule.

questions.stem: A client who believes "more must be better" is considering high-dose antioxidant supplements. For each statement, indicate whether it accurately states a documented risk of high-dose supplementation the nurse should reinforce, or is inaccurate.

rows.t: High-dose vitamin A supplements are linked to increased lung cancer risk in clients who smoke
High-dose vitamin E supplements are linked to hemorrhagic stroke and prostate cancer
High levels of selenium are toxic and can cause severe liver damage
High doses of vitamin C cause gastrointestinal problems such as diarrhea
High-dose antioxidant supplements taken above the RDA are proven to prevent chronic disease

questions.rat: The module ties each megadose to a specific harm: vitamin A and lung cancer in smokers, vitamin E and hemorrhagic stroke plus prostate cancer, selenium and liver toxicity, and vitamin C and GI diarrhea. Research has NOT shown that high-dose supplementation prevents disease, so the final statement is inaccurate and is itself a safety concern.
questions.strat: "More is better" is the trap; each megadose maps to a named toxicity, not a benefit.

questions.stem: During a medication reconciliation, a client taking warfarin reports starting a high-dose vitamin E supplement "for its antioxidant benefits." Which concern should the nurse prioritize?
opts: Vitamin E taken with an anticoagulant raises the client's risk for bleeding
Vitamin E will block the absorption of warfarin and weaken its effect
Vitamin E commonly causes toxicity even at the supplement's usual dose
Vitamin E will raise the client's blood pressure while on the anticoagulant
questions.rat: Vitamin E supplement use is contraindicated with anticoagulant drugs because of bleeding risk, so that is the priority concern. Vitamin E toxicity is otherwise rare, and the source does not describe it blocking warfarin absorption or raising blood pressure.
questions.strat: Antioxidant supplement + anticoagulant in one picture = think additive bleeding risk.

questions.stem: During a wellness visit, a 78-year-old reports new tingling in both feet and trouble remembering recent conversations. The diet recall shows adequate meat and dairy intake. Which question best helps the nurse explore a likely nutritional cause?
opts: "Have you been told you have low stomach acid or take a medicine for reflux?"
"How much time do you spend outdoors in the midday sun each week of the year?"
"Do you add iodized table salt to most of the meals that you prepare?"
"How many servings of citrus fruit and juice do you usually eat each day?"
questions.rat: Older adults lose intrinsic factor and stomach acid, so B12 is poorly absorbed even when dietary intake is adequate; the neurologic (tingling) and cognitive symptoms fit B12 deficiency. Sun exposure points to vitamin D, citrus to vitamin C, and iodized salt to iodine — none of which match this picture.
questions.strat: Adequate intake + absorption symptom = think absorption, not the plate. Older adult + neuro/cognitive = B12.

questions.stem: A client with iron-deficiency anemia asks the nurse to help build an iron-rich grocery list. Classify each food as a good iron source to encourage or a poor iron source to deprioritize for this goal.
questions.rat: Red meat, dark leafy greens, and legumes are the iron sources named in the source material. Milk supplies calcium, which actually competes with iron absorption, and unfortified white bread is not an iron source; fortified grains would be, but plain unfortified bread is not.
questions.strat: Iron = meat, dark greens, beans. Dairy is a calcium food and blocks iron, not a source.

questions.stem: A client is starting a ferrous sulfate supplement for iron-deficiency anemia. Which statements by the client indicate correct understanding of how to take it? Select all that apply.
opts: "I'll take it with a glass of orange juice."
"I'll wait at least 2 hours after my calcium pill or milk before taking it."
"I'll skip my morning coffee right around the time I take it."
"I'll take it together with my calcium supplement so I only swallow one pill at a time."
"I'll drink a large glass of milk with each dose."
questions.rat: Vitamin C (orange juice) increases iron absorption; calcium, milk, and antacids should be separated from iron by at least 2 hours; and caffeine blocks iron absorption. Taking iron together with calcium, or washing it down with milk, reduces absorption and reflects the common error this teaching is meant to prevent.
questions.strat: Iron loves vitamin C; iron fights calcium and caffeine — separate them.

questions.stem: A client taking warfarin tells the nurse, "My friend takes a high-dose vitamin E supplement for her heart, so I bought a bottle too." Which response is most important?
opts: "Vitamin E can add to bleeding risk on warfarin — check with your provider first."
"That's fine — vitamin E is fat-soluble, so your body simply stores any of the extra."
"Be sure to take the vitamin E along with a fatty meal so that it absorbs well."
"Vitamin E will help your body absorb more of the iron that is in your daily diet."
questions.rat: Vitamin E supplementation is contraindicated with anticoagulants because it raises bleeding risk, so the priority is to stop and involve the provider — a safety issue. Reassuring the client, coaching absorption, or citing an iron interaction all ignore the dangerous drug–supplement combination.
questions.strat: On an anticoagulant, screen any supplement for bleeding risk first — vitamin E adds to it.

questions.stem: The nurse reviews four clients in a community clinic. Which client needs the most immediate follow-up?
opts: An adult with a critically abnormal potassium level and an irregular pulse
An older adult asking which everyday foods are the highest in vitamin D
A pregnant client who wants to confirm she is taking enough folate each day
A vegan client asking how to get more iron from the plant foods that she eats
questions.rat: Potassium controls cardiac excitability, and a critical level with an irregular pulse can cause lethal dysrhythmias — an unstable, life-threatening finding that outranks the three teaching needs. The other three are important health-promotion conversations but are stable and not urgent.
questions.strat: Unstable/life-threat beats teaching. Potassium swings = heart = act first.

questions.stem: A postmenopausal client wants to improve calcium absorption to protect her bones. To complete the teaching, the nurse explains that calcium carbonate is best taken [1], and that [2] is also required for the body to absorb calcium.

blanks.label: [1] When to take calcium carbonate
opts: with food
on an empty stomach
with an antacid

blanks.label: [2] Nutrient needed for absorption
opts: vitamin C
vitamin D
vitamin K

questions.rat: Calcium carbonate is best absorbed with food because stomach acid produced during eating aids absorption, and vitamin D is required for calcium absorption. Vitamin C boosts iron, not calcium; vitamin K relates to clotting; and taking it on an empty stomach or with an antacid reduces the acid that carbonate needs.
questions.strat: Carbonate needs acid — take with food. No vitamin D, no calcium uptake.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a community class on how the two vitamin categories behave in the body. For each statement, indicate whether it describes the fat-soluble group (A, D, E, K) or the water-soluble group (B-complex, C).

rows.t: Stored in the liver and fatty tissue for several weeks
Not retained by the body, so it must be taken in regularly
More likely to reach toxic levels when over-supplemented
Absorbed better when eaten with a meal containing some fat
Largely lost into the cooking water when vegetables are boiled

questions.rat: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in fat and the liver, depend on dietary fat for absorption, and accumulate to toxic levels with overuse. Water-soluble vitamins (B-complex, C) are not stored, so they need regular intake, and because they dissolve in water they leach into cooking water during boiling. Linking each behavior to whether the vitamin is stored explains every row.
questions.strat: One word decides each row: 'stored' = fat-soluble; 'not kept / lost in water' = water-soluble.

questions.stem: A client takes daily high-dose supplements of several vitamins, well above the recommended allowances. The nurse explains that some of these vitamins are far more likely than others to build up to toxic levels. Which property best explains why one group carries the greater toxicity risk?
opts: Fat-soluble vitamins are stored in tissue, so excess accumulates rather than being cleared
Fat-soluble vitamins are absorbed faster, so their blood levels spike higher after each daily dose
Water-soluble vitamins bind tightly to plasma proteins, so they linger for weeks in the bloodstream
Water-soluble vitamins are stored in the liver, so the excess slowly collects there over time
questions.rat: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in fat and the liver, so intake above the upper limit accumulates and can cause toxicity, much like a drug's pharmacologic effect. Water-soluble vitamins are not stored and are cleared in the urine, so they rarely reach toxic levels. The absorption-speed and protein-binding options are not the mechanism, and it is the fat-soluble group — not water-soluble — that is stored.
questions.strat: Toxicity follows storage: the vitamins the body holds onto (fat-soluble) are the ones that can build up.

questions.stem: A nurse counsels a client who wants to preserve the water-soluble vitamins (B-complex and C) in the foods they prepare and eat. Which teaching points are appropriate? Select all that apply.
opts: Eat foods rich in these vitamins regularly because the body does not store them
Steam or microwave vegetables rather than boiling them in a large pot of water
Use the leftover cooking water in soups or sauces instead of discarding it
Plan to skip these foods for a few days at a time since reserves last for weeks
Avoid overcooking, since prolonged heat in water destroys and leaches out these vitamins
questions.rat: Because water-soluble vitamins are not stored, regular intake is needed, and because they dissolve in water and are heat-sensitive, gentler methods (steaming, microwaving), reusing cooking water, and avoiding overcooking all reduce losses. The option claiming reserves last for weeks describes fat-soluble storage and is false for this group, so skipping these foods for days risks deficiency.
questions.strat: Water-soluble = dissolves and isn't stored, so the false choice is any that treats them like a stored reserve.

questions.stem: A client on a strict very-low-fat diet asks the nurse how to take a supplement that contains vitamins A, D, E, and K. Which instruction is most appropriate?
opts: Take the supplement with a meal that includes some dietary fat to aid absorption
Take the supplement on an empty stomach with a full glass of water early each morning
Dissolve the supplement in a tall glass of water and drink it slowly between your meals
Take the supplement alone at bedtime, several hours after the last meal of the day
questions.rat: Fat-soluble vitamins require dietary fat to be absorbed, so pairing the supplement with a meal containing some fat improves uptake — especially important when the habitual diet is very low in fat. Taking it on an empty stomach, dissolved in water, or hours away from any fat all limit absorption of this group; those approaches fit water-soluble vitamins instead.
questions.strat: Fat-soluble vitamins need a ride on fat — pair the dose with a meal that has some.

questions.stem: A client says, "My online supplement says it prevents heart disease and lowers cholesterol, and I figure more vitamins can only help." Which response by the nurse is most accurate?
opts: "Supplements are not permitted to claim they prevent disease, and a varied diet is the preferred source"
"That printed claim shows the product was well tested, so raising your daily dose is a reasonable goal"
"Vitamins do not cause harm even in large amounts, so taking extra is a safe way to protect your heart"
"Supplements are regulated just as strictly as prescription drugs, so the labeled health claims are reliable"
questions.rat: Dietary supplements are not intended to diagnose, treat, prevent, or cure disease and cannot legitimately make claims such as preventing heart disease, and the preferred source of vitamins is a variety of whole foods. The other responses are wrong because disease claims do not signal testing, fat-soluble vitamins can reach toxic levels in excess, and supplements are not regulated as strictly as drugs.
questions.strat: Food first, and any supplement promising to 'prevent' or 'cure' disease is overstepping what supplements may claim.

questions.stem: A client who is planning to conceive within the next few months asks the nurse which nutrient is most important to begin before becoming pregnant. Which response by the nurse is most appropriate?
opts: "Increase calcium now so the baby's bones and teeth develop strong and healthy."
"Begin folic acid now to lower the risk of neural tube defects."
"Add extra vitamin C now to help your body absorb the iron you eat each day."
"Take vitamin D now so your body can absorb and use the calcium in your diet."
questions.rat: Neural tube closure occurs in the first 3-4 weeks, often before pregnancy is confirmed, so folic acid must be adequate at conception. Calcium, vitamin C, and vitamin D are all needed in pregnancy but do not prevent neural tube defects, and timing them preconception is not the priority.
questions.strat: Folic acid = neural tube = start BEFORE conception; the early-timing nutrient is the answer.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a pregnant client about foods to avoid to prevent foodborne illness and infection. Which foods should the nurse instruct the client to avoid? Select all that apply.
opts: Cheese made with unpasteurized milk
Sushi and ceviche made with raw fish
Roast beef cooked to a safe internal temperature
Raw sprouts and raw cookie dough
Pasteurized fortified orange juice
Hard-boiled eggs and pasteurized yogurt
questions.rat: Unpasteurized cheese, raw fish, and raw sprouts/dough carry Listeria, Salmonella, or other pathogens that endanger the fetus. Thoroughly cooked roast beef, pasteurized juice, and cooked eggs/pasteurized yogurt are safe and encouraged sources of protein, iron, calcium, and vitamin D — they are distractors because the client may overgeneralize 'avoid meat, juice, eggs, and dairy.'
questions.strat: Raw or unpasteurized = avoid; fully cooked and pasteurized = safe.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching new parents about feeding their healthy infant during the first year. For each practice, indicate whether it is appropriate or should be avoided during the first 12 months.

rows.t: Feeding iron-fortified formula when not breastfeeding
Giving a small taste of honey to soothe the infant
Offering whole cow's milk as the main drink at 8 months
Introducing iron-rich pureed meats around 6 months
Adding extra water to formula to stretch the supply

questions.rat: Iron-fortified formula and iron-rich solids near 6 months meet the infant's high iron needs. Honey is avoided before 1 year (risk of infant botulism), cow's milk before 1 year (poor digestion, low iron and vitamin C), and diluting formula causes water intoxication (hyponatremia, seizures). The 'small taste' and 'main drink' wording models how parents rationalize unsafe practices.
questions.strat: Before age 1: no honey, no cow's milk, never dilute formula.

questions.stem:
CASE (1/6) Recognize cues. A 26-year-old client at 10 weeks gestation comes to the prenatal clinic. The nurse reviews the chart and interview. Which findings should the nurse identify as concerning and requiring follow-up? Select all that apply.

Vital signs: BP 116/72, HR 92, afebrile.
Labs: hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, hematocrit 30%.
History: reports fatigue and feeling 'worn out' climbing stairs.
Diet recall: skips breakfast most days, rarely eats leafy greens or citrus, drinks 3 cups of coffee daily.
Reports she has not yet started a prenatal vitamin.
opts: Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL with hematocrit 30%
Fatigue and shortness of breath on exertion
Diet low in folate sources (leafy greens, citrus)
Has not started a prenatal vitamin
Blood pressure 116/72 mm Hg
Afebrile temperature
questions.rat: Low hemoglobin/hematocrit, exertional fatigue, a folate-poor diet, and no prenatal vitamin are all concerning cues pointing to anemia and inadequate folate intake. The BP and temperature are within normal limits and serve as normal-finding distractors.
questions.strat: Sort each cue normal vs. abnormal on its own; only flag what falls outside expected.

questions.stem: CASE (2/6) Analyze cues. Considering the client's hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL, exertional fatigue, folate-poor diet, and lack of a prenatal vitamin, which interpretation best explains her clinical picture?
opts: Expected hemodilution of pregnancy with no nutritional concern
Iron-deficiency anemia with inadequate folate intake
Gestational diabetes causing fatigue and low energy
Early hyperemesis gravidarum from poor appetite
questions.rat: A hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL with symptoms plus a diet lacking iron- and folate-rich foods points to iron-deficiency anemia with poor folate intake. Physiologic hemodilution lowers values mildly but not to symptomatic 9.8 g/dL; gestational diabetes and hyperemesis are not supported by these cues (no glucose abnormality, no vomiting/weight loss).
questions.strat: Link the cues — low Hgb + symptoms + nutrient-poor diet names the deficiency, not a normal variant.

questions.stem: CASE (3/6) Prioritize hypotheses. The client is now 10 weeks pregnant with confirmed inadequate folate intake and iron-deficiency anemia. Which concern should the nurse prioritize when planning teaching and follow-up?
opts: Inadequate folate intake during ongoing fetal development
Long-term risk of postpartum weight retention after delivery
Reduced calcium intake gradually affecting maternal bone density
Caffeine intake interfering with the client's nighttime sleep
questions.rat: Although neural tube closure peaks earlier, organogenesis continues and the client is still early in pregnancy with a folate-poor diet and untreated anemia, making inadequate folate the highest-priority, time-sensitive fetal risk. Postpartum weight, bone density, and sleep are real but lower-acuity and not the immediate fetal threat.
questions.strat: Prioritize the time-sensitive fetal risk over maternal comfort or long-term concerns.

questions.stem: CASE (4/6) Generate solutions (bowtie). For this client with iron-deficiency anemia and inadequate folate intake, choose the central problem, two nursing actions, and two parameters to monitor.

opts: Iron-deficiency anemia with inadequate folate intake
Gestational diabetes with poor glucose control
Hyperemesis gravidarum with dehydration
Start a prenatal vitamin containing folic acid and iron
Teach iron-rich foods paired with a vitamin C source
Restrict dietary protein to reduce metabolic demand
Encourage an extra glass of milk with the iron dose
Hemoglobin and hematocrit trend
Dietary recall and reported intake
Daily urine ketone strips
Deep tendon reflexes each visit

questions.rat: The problem is iron-deficiency anemia with low folate; appropriate actions are a prenatal vitamin (folic acid + iron) and teaching iron-rich foods with vitamin C to boost absorption. Monitor hemoglobin/hematocrit and dietary intake. Restricting protein and adding milk with iron are plausible-sounding errors (protein is needed; calcium blunts iron). Ketones and reflexes track conditions this client does not have.
questions.strat: Bowtie: name the deficiency, fix it (supplement + absorption-smart diet), then track the labs and the intake you changed.

questions.stem: CASE (5/6) Take action. The nurse provides teaching for the client's iron-deficiency anemia and low folate intake. Which instructions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
opts: Take the prenatal vitamin daily, as it supplies folic acid and iron
Eat folate sources such as leafy greens and citrus fruits
Take the iron with orange juice rather than with milk
Take the iron together with a calcium supplement at each meal
Replace meals with extra coffee on days you feel too tired to cook
Increase your daily coffee to four or more cups to help with the fatigue
questions.rat: A prenatal vitamin, folate-rich foods, and iron taken with vitamin C directly address the deficiencies. Taking iron with calcium reduces absorption; substituting coffee for meals worsens intake; and increasing caffeine further impairs iron absorption — all realistic but wrong choices a fatigued client might make.
questions.strat: Keep actions that raise iron/folate and aid absorption; drop anything that competes with iron or skips meals.

questions.stem: CASE (6/6) Evaluate outcomes. The client returns 6 weeks after starting a prenatal vitamin and dietary changes. Which finding best indicates that the plan has been effective?
opts: Hemoglobin has risen to 11.4 g/dL and she now eats greens, citrus, and lean meats daily
Hemoglobin is 10.0 g/dL and she takes the prenatal vitamin only a few days each week
She stopped the iron because it caused constipation and now feels far less bloated overall
Hemoglobin is unchanged, but she reports less fatigue after raising her daily coffee intake
questions.rat: A hemoglobin rise toward normal plus a consistently iron- and folate-rich diet shows the plan worked. The other options are realistic partial or false improvements: inconsistent dosing barely moved the value, stopping iron for a side effect undermines treatment, and feeling better from more caffeine masks an unchanged lab while worsening iron absorption.
questions.strat: Success = the lab improved AND the behavior that fixed it is sustained; feeling better alone isn't proof.

questions.stem: A mother of a 3-year-old tells the nurse, "He used to eat everything, but now he only wants crackers and refuses dinner. I'm worried he isn't getting enough." The child's growth is tracking steadily along the 50th percentile. Which response by the nurse is most appropriate?
opts: "Keep offering a variety of foods and let him decide how much to eat."
"Have him stay at the table until he finishes everything on his plate."
"Give him a daily children's multivitamin so the missed meals don't matter."
"Offer his favorite crackers between meals so he takes in enough calories."
questions.rat: Food jags and reduced intake are normal as growth slows after toddlerhood; the parent should offer variety and let the child self-regulate amount, since steady growth shows needs are met. Forcing a child to clean the plate undermines self-regulation, a vitamin does not fix an eating pattern, and grazing on a preferred snack between meals reinforces the jag and spoils appetite for balanced meals.
questions.strat: Steady growth + normal toddler pickiness = reassure and offer variety; don't force, bribe, or graze.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching the parents of a 2-year-old about preventing choking. Which foods should the nurse identify as choking hazards that need to be modified or avoided for this child? Select all that apply.
opts: Whole grapes
Hot dog slices cut into rounds
Popcorn
Whole nuts
Mashed sweet potato
Plain whole-milk yogurt
questions.rat: Round, firm, or hard foods that match the size of a toddler's airway are the classic high-risk choking foods: whole grapes, hot dogs cut into rounds (which form a plug), popcorn, and whole nuts. Grapes and hot dogs should be cut lengthwise into small pieces. Mashed sweet potato and yogurt are soft, smooth textures that pose no choking risk.
questions.strat: Choking risk = round, firm, slippery, airway-sized — grapes, hot dogs, nuts, popcorn; soft/mashed is safe.

questions.stem: The nurse reviews a typical school day for an 8-year-old: skips breakfast, drinks two 12-oz sodas, eats a school lunch, and has chips after school. The parent says the child is often tired and inattentive at school. Which dietary change should the nurse recommend addressing first?
opts: Start a daily breakfast and swap the sodas for water or milk
Add a fiber supplement to the diet to improve bowel regularity
Switch the salted after-school chips to baked vegetable chips
Begin a children's chewable calcium tablet every morning
questions.rat: Eating breakfast daily is linked to better academic performance and behavior, and sugar-sweetened beverages are a leading source of empty calories in children, so adding breakfast and cutting the sodas targets the two highest-yield problems explaining the fatigue and inattention. A fiber supplement, a chip swap, and a calcium tablet address lower-priority or unrelated concerns and do not fix the missed morning meal or the sugar load.
questions.strat: School-age red flags: skipped breakfast + sugary drinks — fix those before fine-tuning snacks.

questions.stem: The nurse counsels a 15-year-old female who is in her growth spurt. To support building bone mass, the priority nutrients are calcium and [1]. Because she is menstruating, the nurse also emphasizes adequate [2] intake to support growth, blood volume, and immunity.

blanks.label: [1] Bone-building partner nutrient
opts: vitamin C
vitamin D
vitamin K

blanks.label: [2] Nutrient especially needed by menstruating females
opts: iron
zinc
folate

questions.rat: Calcium and vitamin D together are critical during adolescence because nearly all bone mass is laid down by age 18; vitamin D drives calcium absorption. Menstruating adolescent females are at elevated risk for iron deficiency, and iron supports growth, blood volume, immunity, and cognitive development. Vitamin C, K, zinc, and folate matter generally but are not the targeted answers here.
questions.strat: Teen bone mass = calcium + vitamin D; menstruating teen = watch iron.

questions.stem: A school nurse evaluates a 16-year-old gymnast whose mother reports concerns about her eating. For each finding, indicate whether it is an Expected adolescent finding or a Concerning finding that warrants further assessment for a possible eating disorder.

rows.t: Increased appetite during a growth spurt
Going to the bathroom immediately after most meals
Wearing baggy layers and refusing to eat in front of others
Has stopped menstruating despite continued intense training
Occasionally choosing a vegetarian meal at lunch

questions.rat: Increased appetite during a growth spurt and an occasional vegetarian meal are normal. Self-induced behaviors and warning signs of an eating disorder include disappearing to the bathroom after eating (possible purging), concealing the body and avoiding eating around others, and amenorrhea from low energy availability, which is a recognized complication of weight-control practices in lean-sport athletes. These warrant nonjudgmental further assessment.
questions.strat: Eating-disorder red flags: bathroom after meals, hiding the body, amenorrhea — appetite and a veggie lunch are normal.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old has a BMI in the 95th percentile for age and sex. The parents ask the nurse how to help. Which approach best reflects evidence-based, family-centered teaching for childhood overweight?
opts: Have the whole family adopt healthier meals and active routines together
Put the child on a calorie-restricted diet separate from the rest of the family
Tell the child to weigh daily and record everything that is eaten
Limit the child to one meal a day until the weight comes down
questions.rat: Family-based interventions are the evidence-based approach: the family environment shapes a child's eating attitudes and behaviors, so changing meals and activity for the whole household supports the child without shaming or singling them out. Isolating the child on a restrictive diet, mandating daily self-weighing and food logging, and skipping meals are stigmatizing or unsafe and can promote disordered eating in a growing child.
questions.strat: Childhood obesity teaching is family-wide and non-shaming — never isolate or starve the child.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old client with a sedentary lifestyle and a BMI in the overweight range says, "I want to eat better to lower my risk of heart disease and diabetes." Using the Dietary Guidelines for Americans, which change should the nurse recommend the client focus on first?
opts: Eat more produce while cutting back on refined grains, added sugar, and sodium
Cut nearly all dietary fat and replace those calories with extra grains and bread
Switch to a very high-protein plan and skip one meal each day to force a deficit
Add a daily multivitamin and otherwise keep the current eating pattern unchanged
questions.rat: The Guidelines emphasize small shifts toward nutrient-dense foods, more produce, and less refined grains, added sugar, and sodium to lower diet-related chronic disease risk. Eliminating all fat and piling on grains misapplies the guidance; skipping meals on a high-protein plan is a fad approach, not a balanced pattern; a supplement does not fix an unbalanced base diet.
questions.strat: Chronic-disease prevention = shift the whole eating pattern toward nutrient density, not one magic fix.

questions.topic: Older Adult Energy vs. Nutrient Needs
questions.stem: The nurse teaches a 77-year-old client about age-related nutrition changes. As a person ages, total daily calorie needs generally [1], while the need for most vitamins and minerals [2]. Because of this combination, the nurse should counsel the client to prioritize [3].

blanks.label: [1] Calorie needs
opts: decrease
increase
stay the highest of any life stage

blanks.label: [2] Nutrient needs
opts: fall sharply as well
stay the same or increase
no longer apply

blanks.label: [3] Counseling focus
opts: nutrient-dense foods over empty calories
larger total portion sizes
extra refined grains for energy

questions.rat: With aging, metabolism and energy needs decline while requirements for most nutrients stay the same or rise, so each calorie must carry more nutrition; nutrient-dense foods meet needs without excess calories. Larger portions or refined grains add calories without proportional nutrients, widening the mismatch.
questions.strat: Aging = fewer calories, same/more nutrients, so every bite has to count (nutrient density).

questions.stem: An 80-year-old client living alone is admitted with new confusion, dry mucous membranes, and dark concentrated urine. The client says, "I just don't feel thirsty anymore, so I forget to drink." Which age-related change best explains this client's risk?
opts: A blunted thirst sensation that lets fluid deficits build unnoticed
A rising filtration rate that steadily flushes out body water and salt
A heightened thirst drive that pushes the client toward overhydration
An elevated metabolic rate that raises the client's baseline fluid needs
questions.rat: Older adults have a blunted thirst sensation, so they under-drink and slip into dehydration before noticing, which matches this client's findings. Glomerular filtration falls with age rather than rising, thirst is decreased rather than heightened, and metabolic rate declines rather than climbing.
questions.strat: Old + confused + dry membranes = think dehydration; the missing thirst cue is the trap.

questions.topic: Malnutrition Risk Factors in Older Adults
questions.stem: A community-health nurse screens older adults living independently for malnutrition risk. Which findings should the nurse identify as increasing an older adult's risk for malnutrition? Select all that apply.
opts: Lives alone and reports eating most meals in isolation
Relies on a fixed income and skips food to afford other bills
Has several missing teeth and difficulty chewing many foods
Takes multiple medications that cause dry mouth and low appetite
Walks daily with neighbors and shares cooked meals on weekends
Keeps the home stocked with a variety of fresh and frozen foods
questions.rat: Social isolation, food insecurity on a fixed income, chewing problems, and polypharmacy with xerostomic or appetite-suppressing side effects all reduce intake and raise malnutrition risk in older adults. Regular social meals with neighbors and a well-stocked variety of food are protective, not risk factors.
questions.strat: Elder malnutrition is mostly social and functional: isolation, money, teeth, meds. The two protective options are the odd ones out.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old client asks which nutrients older adults most often fall short on, given the age-related decline in nutrient absorption and synthesis. The nurse's teaching should highlight which pair as commonly inadequate in this age group?
opts: Vitamin B12 and vitamin D
Vitamin C and vitamin K
The minerals sodium and chloride
The B vitamins niacin and biotin
questions.rat: Aging reduces the ability to absorb and synthesize nutrients, leaving older adults at particular risk for inadequate vitamin B12 and vitamin D. Vitamins C and K, niacin/biotin, and sodium/chloride are not the classic at-risk nutrients of aging, and sodium is more often consumed in excess.
questions.strat: Older-adult deficiency pair to memorize: B12 and D.

questions.stem: A nurse cares for an older client with Parkinson's disease who coughs and has a wet, gurgly voice after sips of thin tea. The client lives alone and prefers soft foods. Which action should the nurse prioritize?
opts: Request a professional swallowing evaluation before more oral intake
Encourage extra thin liquids between meals so the client stays hydrated
Have the client recline at a 30-degree angle while eating to ease swallowing
Add hard raw vegetables to meals to help strengthen the chewing muscles
questions.rat: Coughing and a wet, gurgly voice after thin liquids are warning signs of dysphagia and aspiration risk; a swallowing evaluation guides safe texture modification and must come before more oral intake (safety, assess-before-intervene). Pushing thin liquids, reclining while eating, and hard raw foods each increase aspiration or choking risk.
questions.strat: Wet voice + cough after liquids = dysphagia red flag; stop and get a swallow eval before feeding more.

questions.stem:
A home care nurse assesses a three-generation household at one visit. Which family member needs the nurse's follow-up FIRST for a current nutrition-related problem?

- A 33-year-old who is 9 weeks pregnant and continues eating sushi and sashimi made with raw fish
- An 8-year-old who avoids all dairy products but is offered other foods at meals
- An 11-year-old who eats fruit and carrots but refuses most other vegetables
- A 34-year-old in good health who eats a varied, balanced diet
opts: The 33-year-old who is 9 weeks pregnant and eating raw fish
The 8-year-old who avoids all dairy but is offered other foods
The 11-year-old who refuses most vegetables but does eat fruit
The 34-year-old healthy parent who eats a balanced, varied diet
questions.rat: Raw fish in pregnancy carries a real, immediate risk to mother and fetus (Listeria, mercury, parasites), so the pregnant client with an active harmful behavior is the current problem needing follow-up first. The dairy-avoiding child and the vegetable-refusing child are at risk for deficiency over time but are still being offered adequate alternatives, and the healthy parent has no nutritional concern.
questions.strat: Acute, active harm to two clients (mother + fetus) outranks a slow-developing single-nutrient risk.

questions.stem: A client who is 9 weeks pregnant tells the nurse that sushi, poke, and sashimi are her favorite foods and part of her family's culture. Which response by the nurse is most appropriate?
opts: "Let's find cooked versions of those dishes so you can keep your traditions safely."
"You will need to give up those favorite dishes entirely until well after the baby is born."
"Those raw dishes are fine in small amounts as long as you eat them only about once a week."
"Eating raw fish is a personal choice, so keep enjoying the dishes if they matter to you."
questions.rat: Effective teaching pairs the safety rule (avoid raw fish in pregnancy) with the client's culture by adapting dishes to cooked versions, supporting both adherence and cultural identity. Telling her to give up the dishes entirely is unnecessary since cooked versions are safe; permitting small or weekly amounts of raw fish is still unsafe; and endorsing continued raw fish ignores the fetal risk.
questions.strat: Best teaching keeps the client's culture while changing only the unsafe part - adapt, don't forbid.

questions.stem: The nurse plans nutrition teaching tailored to each household member's life stage and situation. Indicate whether each strategy is Appropriate or Not appropriate as written.

rows.t: For the 82-year-old with moderate dementia, serve only 1 to 2 foods at a time and allow extra time to eat
For the 8-year-old who avoids dairy, offer calcium-fortified foods such as orange juice and bread
For the 88-year-old with hypertension, teach the DASH eating plan and adapt it to his cultural dishes
For the 11-year-old who dislikes vegetables, withhold preferred fruits until she eats her vegetables
For the 88-year-old with hypertension, advise him to salt foods to taste since intake naturally drops with age

questions.rat: Simplifying meals and allowing extra time fits dementia care; fortified foods meet the dairy-avoiding child's calcium needs; and culturally adapted DASH teaching supports the older adult with hypertension. Withholding preferred foods as leverage creates power struggles and is not recommended for children, and encouraging added salt directly contradicts the low-sodium goal for hypertension.
questions.strat: Tailor to the stage: simplify for dementia, fortify for the picky eater, and never use food as a bargaining chip with kids.

questions.stem: An 88-year-old client with hypertension wears full dentures and is starting the DASH eating plan. Which actions should the nurse include to support his nutrition and adherence? Select all that apply.
opts: Emphasize fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy
Adapt the low-sodium plan to his cultural food preferences
Arrange a dental evaluation to refit his dentures for chewing
Teach that red meats and sweets are allowed in small amounts
Have him eliminate all dairy products to lower sodium intake
Tell him to add table salt to improve the taste of bland foods
questions.rat: DASH emphasizes fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy while allowing red meat and sweets in small amounts, and adapting it to culture improves adherence. Refitting dentures supports chewing and intake in an older adult. Eliminating all dairy is wrong - low-fat dairy is a DASH component - and adding table salt undermines the low-sodium goal.
questions.strat: DASH limits sodium, not dairy; fix the dentures so the older adult can actually eat the plan.

questions.stem: Six months after nutrition teaching, the nurse evaluates a pregnant client's progress. She gained 4 pounds in the first trimester, then about 1 pound per week, for a total of 33 pounds from her pre-pregnancy weight, and she avoids raw fish. Which conclusion is best supported?
opts: Gestational weight gain and food safety are within expected limits; reinforce current habits
The total weight gain is excessive and the client should restrict calories for the rest of the pregnancy
The first-trimester gain was inadequate and indicates poor fetal growth that needs urgent referral
Weight gain is acceptable but raw-fish avoidance shows the teaching about safe foods failed
questions.rat: A modest first-trimester gain followed by roughly 1 pound per week, totaling about 33 pounds, falls within the normal range for a single pregnancy, and avoiding raw fish shows the safety teaching worked - so the plan is effective and should be reinforced. The total is not excessive, the early gain is normal rather than a growth emergency, and avoiding raw fish is the desired outcome, not a failure.
questions.strat: On an outcome question, compare the data to the expected pattern before calling it a problem - normal weight gain plus a safe diet equals success.

questions.stem:
A community nurse reviews recommended daily iron intake across several clients: a 2-year-old toddler (7 mg), a 16-year-old female (15 mg), a 30-year-old pregnant client (27 mg), and a 70-year-old man (8 mg).

Which client has the highest recommended iron intake, and what best explains it?
opts: The pregnant client, because iron must support expanded maternal blood volume and fetal needs
The 16-year-old female, because the pubertal growth spurt is the period of greatest iron demand
The toddler, because rapid early growth makes iron the limiting nutrient for development
The 70-year-old man, because slowed peristalsis reduces iron absorption with age
questions.rat: Pregnancy carries the highest recommended iron intake (27 mg) because iron supports the expanded maternal blood volume and the developing fetus. The adolescent female (15 mg) is high but second; the toddler (7 mg) is comparatively low; and reduced absorption in older adults raises intake needs in theory but the recommendation actually drops to 8 mg.
questions.strat: When a need peaks, ask what physiologic demand is being added — pregnancy adds a whole second circulation.

questions.stem: A nurse teaches that nutritional priorities shift across the lifespan because the body's demands change. Recommended iron intake for females is [1] from the toddler years to mid-adolescence, and the recommendation for a non-pregnant woman then [2] after menopause. The factor that most directly drives the higher need during the reproductive years is [3].

blanks.label: [1] Toddler → adolescence
opts: roughly doubles
stays the same
is cut in half

blanks.label: [2] After menopause
opts: rises sharply
falls back toward childhood levels
is unchanged

blanks.label: [3] Main driver in reproductive years
opts: menstrual iron losses
declining stomach acid
lower physical activity

questions.rat: Female iron need rises from 7 mg (toddler) to 15 mg (14–18 yr), roughly doubling, then drops to 8 mg at age 51+ as menstrual losses end. The elevated requirement through the reproductive years is driven chiefly by recurring menstrual blood loss, not by acid changes or activity level.
questions.strat: Track a single nutrient up and down the age columns — the inflection points reveal the physiology.

questions.stem:
A nurse plans foodborne-illness teaching for a community center serving people of all ages. Which individuals should be identified as being at HIGHER risk for serious foodborne illness and prioritized for teaching?

Select all that apply.
opts: A child under 5 years of age
An adult aged 65 and older
A client receiving chemotherapy
A client who is pregnant
A healthy, active 25-year-old
questions.rat: The four higher-risk groups span the lifespan and share weakened or altered defenses: young children (developing immunity, less stomach acid), older adults (declining immunity, slowed peristalsis, reduced liver/kidney clearance), the immunocompromised, and pregnant clients (altered immunity plus fetal risk). A healthy young adult is not in a designated high-risk group.
questions.strat: Foodborne risk clusters at the extremes of age and wherever defenses are altered — pregnancy and immune suppression.

questions.stem:
A nurse is developing food-asphyxiation prevention materials and explains that choking risk is not evenly distributed across the lifespan.

Which statement best reflects how this risk is distributed?
opts: Both toddlers and older adults face elevated risk, from different causes
School-age children carry the highest risk, which then declines with age
Only toddlers are at real risk, since adults chew and swallow maturely
Risk climbs steadily with advancing age and peaks in middle adulthood
questions.rat: Choking can occur at any age, but risk concentrates at two ends of the lifespan: toddlers, whose chewing and swallowing skills are still developing, and older adults, in whom advanced age and coexisting conditions raise both the likelihood and the lethality of an event. It is not limited to toddlers, nor does it peak in school-age children or middle adulthood.
questions.strat: When risk is 'U-shaped' across age, name the distinct mechanism at each end rather than picking one group.

questions.stem:
A nurse refers clients to federal nutrition assistance programs. A low-income client who is pregnant asks which program is designed specifically for her life stage and that of her future infant.

Which program is the most appropriate referral?
opts: Women, Infants, and Children (WIC), which targets pregnancy and early childhood
The National School Lunch Program (NSLP), which serves enrolled school-age children
The Emergency Food Assistance Program (TEFAP), distributed through state food banks
Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefits for grocery purchases
questions.rat: WIC is the program targeted to a specific lifespan window — low-income pregnant, breastfeeding, and postpartum women, plus infants and children up to age 5 at nutritional risk — providing supplemental foods, referrals, and nutrition education. NSLP serves school-age children, while SNAP and TEFAP address food insecurity broadly without targeting this stage.
questions.strat: Match the program to the life stage in its name — WIC literally names Women, Infants, and Children.

questions.stem:
During the anthropometric portion of a nutritional assessment, the nurse reviews an older adult's weight history. The client weighed 60 kg one month ago and weighs 54 kg today.

Enter the percent of weight change over the past month (round to the nearest whole number).
questions.rat: Percent weight change = weight lost ÷ original weight × 100 = 6 ÷ 60 × 100 = 10%. Percent of weight change over a given time period is the anthropometric data point the guide lists for the 'A' of the ABCDs, and an unintentional loss of this size flags the client for further nutritional evaluation.
questions.strat: Always divide by the ORIGINAL (earlier) weight, not the current weight, when computing percent change.

questions.stem:
The nurse is monitoring the protein status of a client receiving a nutrition intervention. Serial biochemical (laboratory) values are recorded:

• Admission: albumin 2.6 g/dL, prealbumin 9 mg/dL
• Week 2: albumin 3.0 g/dL, prealbumin 14 mg/dL
• Week 4: albumin 3.4 g/dL, prealbumin 19 mg/dL

Which interpretation of this trend is most accurate?
opts: The protein markers are rising over time, so the nutrition intervention is improving protein status
The protein markers are falling over time, so the client's protein status is steadily worsening
The values are all within normal limits, so no nutritional concern was present on admission
The values mainly reflect kidney function rather than protein status and therefore should not be trended
questions.rat: Albumin and prealbumin are the protein markers the guide places in the biochemical ('B') category, used to determine nutritional deficiencies; tracking them over time is an anthropometric/biochemical evaluation outcome. Both values rise steadily across the three timepoints, indicating improving protein status and a working intervention. They are not falling, were clearly low on admission, and are protein — not renal — markers.
questions.strat: On a trend item, read the direction across ALL timepoints before judging — a steady rise in protein markers means the intervention is working.

questions.stem: A client is receiving parenteral nutrition through a peripherally-inserted central catheter (PICC) line. Which complication should the nurse recognize as the most common and serious risk of this therapy?
opts: Catheter-related infection at the central line
Aspiration of the nutrition solution into the lungs
Clogging of the feeding tube lumen
Abdominal distention and cramping
questions.rat: The guide states infection of the catheter is the most common and serious complication of parenteral nutrition, which is why sterile technique is required for insertion and maintenance of the central line. Aspiration, tube clogging, and abdominal distention are complications of enteral (tube) feeding, which uses the GI tract — not of parenteral nutrition, which bypasses the GI system and is infused into the bloodstream.
questions.strat: Match the complication to the route: a line in the bloodstream worries you about infection; a tube in the gut worries you about aspiration and GI upset.

questions.stem:
CASE (1/6) Recognize cues. An 82-year-old client with dementia and difficulty swallowing is receiving a continuous nasogastric (NG) tube feeding. Two hours into the shift the nurse gathers the following data.

Which findings are relevant cues the nurse should note as the assessment proceeds? Select all that apply.
opts: The client is reporting nausea and the abdomen appears distended
The head of the bed has been lowered flat for linen changes and not raised again
The client has vomited a small amount of tube-feeding formula
The feedings are being counted as intake on the intake and output (I&O) record
A variety of enteral formulas are commercially available for tube feeding
The tube was placed because the client's upper GI tract is only partially functioning
questions.rat: Nausea, abdominal distention, and vomiting are GI complications of enteral feeding the guide lists, and a flat head of bed is a setup for aspiration during feeding — all are relevant cues. Counting feedings as I&O, the availability of many formulas, and the indication for tube placement are true background facts from the guide but are not abnormal cues that change the picture for this client.
questions.strat: A 'cue' is data that is abnormal or changes the plan — separate it from true-but-routine background information.

questions.stem: CASE (2/6) Analyze cues. The nurse links the cues together: the client has nausea, abdominal distention, vomited formula, and the head of the bed is flat. Which explanation best accounts for this cluster of findings?
opts: Feeding intolerance with GI complications and aspiration risk
A catheter-related bloodstream infection from the central feeding access
An expected response to continuous enteral feeding that needs no change
Dehydration caused by inadequate free-water intake through the tube
questions.rat: Nausea, distention, and vomiting are the GI complications of tube feeding the guide names, and with the head of the bed flat the vomited formula puts the client at risk for aspiration — together this is feeding intolerance. The access is an NG tube, not a central catheter, so bloodstream infection does not fit; vomiting formula is not an expected finding; and dehydration would not explain the nausea and distention.
questions.strat: Cluster the cues before naming the problem — GI upset plus a flat bed during feeding points to intolerance with aspiration risk.

questions.stem: CASE (3/6) Prioritize hypotheses. Considering the client's vomiting of formula with the head of the bed flat, which concern should the nurse rank as the highest priority?
opts: Aspiration of formula into the airway
Diarrhea from the enteral formula
Clogging of the nasogastric tube
Slowed progress toward the client's weight goal
questions.rat: The guide repeatedly stresses positioning to prevent aspiration, and aspiration is the mechanical complication that threatens the airway — by an airway/safety framework it outranks the others. Diarrhea, a clogged tube, and slow weight gain are real enteral concerns from the guide but none is an immediate threat to airway or breathing.
questions.strat: When one hypothesis threatens the airway, it wins the priority — aspiration outranks GI tolerance and equipment issues.

questions.stem: CASE (4/6) Generate solutions (bowtie). Choose the central problem, two actions, and two parameters to monitor for this client — all drawn from safe enteral feeding practice.

opts: Feeding intolerance with aspiration risk
Catheter-related bloodstream infection
Deficient fluid volume from diuresis
Place the client in a semi-Fowler's position during and after the feeding
Verify tube placement before giving any feeding or medication
Increase the feeding rate to meet the daily calorie goal sooner
Flush the tube with the parenteral solution to clear it
Gastric residual amount
Nausea, vomiting, and abdominal distention
Central line insertion site for redness
Head circumference

questions.rat: The problem is feeding intolerance with aspiration risk. Safe enteral care from the guide means keeping the client in semi-Fowler's during and after feeding and verifying placement before instilling anything, while monitoring gastric residual (to prevent over-inflation of the stomach) and the GI tolerance signs of nausea, vomiting, and distention. A central-line site and head circumference belong to other situations, and raising the rate or flushing with parenteral solution are unsafe distractors.
questions.strat: In the bowtie, the actions fix the problem and the parameters watch the same problem — keep both columns tied to aspiration and tolerance.

questions.stem: CASE (5/6) Take action. The nurse intervenes to manage the feeding intolerance and aspiration risk. Which actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.
opts: Keep the head of the bed elevated in semi-Fowler's during and after the feeding
Verify correct tube placement before resuming the feeding or giving medications
Check the gastric residual to prevent over-inflation of the stomach
Lay the client flat to make them more comfortable during the feeding
Continue the current rate without reassessing because feedings count as intake
questions.rat: Semi-Fowler's positioning during and after feeding, verifying placement before instilling anything, and checking gastric residual to prevent over-inflation are the safe-care steps the guide specifies for enteral feeding. Laying the client flat increases aspiration risk, and continuing unchanged ignores the intolerance — both are realistic but unsafe errors.
