questions.strat: For tube-feeding safety, sit them up, prove the tube is in the right place, and check residual — the three steps the guide repeats.

questions.stem: CASE (6/6) Evaluate outcomes. After the nurse's interventions, which reassessment finding best indicates that the plan for this client is working?
opts: The client now tolerates the feeding with no nausea, vomiting, or distention
The client still reports mild nausea, although the abdomen is no longer distended
The feeding is now infusing at a noticeably faster rate than it was before
The gastric residual has increased compared with the value at the previous check
questions.rat: Resolution of the GI complications (no nausea, vomiting, or distention) with the client kept upright shows the feeding intolerance and aspiration risk are resolving — a monitoring-and-evaluation outcome from the guide. Persistent nausea is only a partial improvement, a faster rate does not show tolerance, and a rising residual signals worsening rather than improvement.
questions.strat: Judge the outcome against the original problem — the goal was tolerating the feeding safely, so look for the cues to have cleared, not just partly eased.

questions.stem: A client recovering from abdominal surgery is tolerating sips of water and is ready to begin oral intake. The nurse reviews the consistency progression. The first oral diet, used to maintain fluid balance with minimal digestive impact, allows [1]. As the client improves and is advanced to the next step, the diet may add [2].

blanks.label: [1] Clear liquid allowed item
opts: beef bouillon and gelatin
strained cream of mushroom soup
vanilla pudding

blanks.label: [2] First item added on full liquid
opts: scrambled eggs
smooth ice cream and custard
buttered white toast

questions.rat: A clear liquid diet allows liquids you can see through — broth, bouillon, gelatin, clear juice — to maintain fluid balance after surgery or vomiting. A full liquid diet is the next step and adds smooth dairy items such as ice cream, custards, and strained cream soups. Cream soup and pudding are full-liquid, not clear; eggs and toast are solids that belong to a soft or regular diet, several steps later.
questions.strat: If you can see through it, it's a clear liquid. Full liquid adds smooth dairy, still nothing you chew.

questions.stem: A client is being advanced from a clear liquid diet back toward a regular diet after an episode of acute gastroenteritis. Which sequence reflects the correct order of diet advancement?
opts: Clear liquid, then full liquid, then pureed/mechanical soft, then regular
Clear liquid, then pureed/mechanical soft, then full liquid, then regular
Full liquid, then clear liquid, then regular, then pureed/mechanical soft
Clear liquid, then regular, then full liquid, then pureed/mechanical soft
questions.rat: Diets are advanced from least to most digestive workload: clear liquid (see-through) to full liquid (smooth dairy added) to pureed/mechanical soft (food that needs little or no chewing) to regular. The other sequences place a thicker or harder-to-digest texture before an easier one, which is not how tolerance is built.
questions.strat: Advance thin-to-thick, easy-to-hard: clear, full, soft/pureed, regular.

questions.stem: A client who had a stroke was evaluated by speech therapy and placed on a mildly thick (IDDSI Level 2) liquid and minced-and-moist (Level 5) food diet for dysphagia. At lunch the nurse observes the client coughing and clearing the throat after each sip of the thickened juice and notices a wet, gurgly voice. Which action should the nurse take first?
opts: Stop the oral intake and keep the client upright to protect the airway
Thin the juice with extra water so it slides down more easily for the client
Encourage the client to drink faster to clear the liquid through quickly
Switch the client to thin liquids since the thick juice causes coughing
questions.rat: Coughing, throat-clearing, and a wet/gurgly voice are signs the thickened liquid is being aspirated; the priority is to stop intake and protect the airway (ABCs) before reassessing. Thinning the liquid or moving to thin liquids increases aspiration risk in dysphagia, and urging faster drinking worsens it.
questions.strat: Wet voice + coughing on liquids = aspiration. Stop feeding, protect the airway first; thin liquids are riskier, not safer.

questions.topic: Renal Diet — Foods Allowed vs. Avoid
questions.stem: A client on hemodialysis is learning to choose foods within a renal diet that limits potassium and phosphorus. For each food, classify it as one to encourage or one to limit/avoid on this diet.
questions.rat: Apples, berries, grapes, broccoli, carrots, and green beans are the lower-potassium choices the National Kidney Foundation lists to encourage. Bananas and orange juice are high-potassium fruit; dairy is high in phosphorus; potatoes and tomatoes are high-potassium vegetables; and salt substitutes are avoided because they contain potassium. All four are limited on a renal diet.
questions.strat: Renal diet limits potassium and phosphorus: dairy = phosphorus; banana, OJ, potato, tomato, salt substitute = potassium. Apples/berries/grapes and broccoli/carrots/green beans are the safe swaps.

questions.stem: A client with heart failure and hypertension is being taught a low-sodium diet that pairs with the DASH approach. Which client statements indicate correct understanding? Select all that apply.
opts: "I'll season my food with herbs and spices instead of salt."
"I'll read the nutrition label, since most of my sodium comes from processed and prepared foods."
"I'll build meals around fruits, vegetables, and low-fat dairy."
"I'll switch from fresh chicken to canned ravioli and frozen dinners for convenience."
"Adding soy sauce instead of salt is a good low-sodium swap."
questions.rat: Using herbs/spices, reading labels (about 77% of sodium is from processed/prepared foods), and centering meals on fruits, vegetables, and low-fat dairy are all consistent with a low-sodium DASH pattern. Canned entrees and frozen dinners are named high-sodium foods, and soy sauce is explicitly high in sodium — both are common errors, not improvements.
questions.strat: Low sodium = fresh, label-read, herbs not salt. Canned/frozen/soy sauce are the hidden-salt traps.

questions.stem: A client newly diagnosed with celiac disease asks the nurse which foods may contain hidden gluten. Which items should the nurse tell the client to check labels on or avoid? Select all that apply.
opts: Soy sauce
Beer
Canned soups and gravies
Plain grilled chicken breast
Fresh apples and grapes
questions.rat: Soy sauce, beer, and canned soups/gravies are listed as common sources of hidden gluten because they contain or are processed with wheat, barley, or malt. Plain chicken and fresh fruit are naturally gluten-free whole foods and do not require label-checking for gluten, so flagging them reflects over-restriction rather than the actual hidden-gluten risk.
questions.strat: Hidden gluten hides in processed liquids and sauces — soy sauce, beer, soups, gravy. Naturally single-ingredient whole foods are safe.

questions.stem: A client with type 2 diabetes is using the Diabetes Plate Method and shows the nurse a photo of dinner: half the 9-inch plate is fried chicken, one quarter is white rice, and one quarter is buttered corn, with a regular soda. Which adjustment best aligns the meal with the plate method?
opts: Fill half the plate with non-starchy vegetables and swap the soda for water
Remove the protein entirely and replace that quarter with a second serving of rice
Double the rice and corn so the plate is mostly carbohydrate for energy
Keep the plate as is but add a large glass of fruit juice on the side
questions.rat: The Diabetes Plate Method fills half the plate with non-starchy vegetables, one quarter with lean protein, one quarter with carbohydrate, and pairs it with water or a low-calorie drink. This meal has no non-starchy vegetables and a sugary soda, so adding vegetables and switching to water is the correct fix. Removing protein, doubling carbohydrate, or adding juice all worsen the carbohydrate-to-vegetable balance and raise blood glucose.
questions.strat: Plate method: half non-starchy veggies, quarter lean protein, quarter carbs, water to drink.

questions.stem: Two clients have new diet orders. Client A has an inflamed, irritated bowel after recent surgery; Client B is managing chronic constipation and hemorrhoids. Which plan correctly matches each diet to the client?
opts: Client A on a low-fiber (low-residue) diet; Client B on a high-fiber diet with more fluids
Client A on a high-fiber diet with more fluids; Client B on a low-fiber (low-residue) diet
Both clients on a high-fiber diet to keep the bowel moving
Client A on a high-fiber diet with a fluid restriction; Client B on a low-residue diet
questions.rat: A low-fiber/low-residue diet reduces stool bulk to rest an inflamed or post-surgical bowel, so it fits Client A; a high-fiber diet with at least eight glasses of fluid daily prevents constipation and hemorrhoids, so it fits Client B. Reversing them, or giving everyone high fiber, irritates the healing bowel; high fiber is also contraindicated with a fluid restriction.
questions.strat: Irritated/post-op bowel = rest it with low residue. Constipation/hemorrhoids = high fiber plus fluids. Never pair high fiber with a fluid restriction.

questions.stem: A client newly started on warfarin tells the nurse, "I eat a big spinach-and-kale salad most days, but I'll just cut all leafy greens out completely now." Which response by the nurse is most appropriate?
opts: "Try to keep your leafy-green intake about the same amount from day to day."
"Cutting greens out of your diet is the safest plan now that you take warfarin."
"Eat extra leafy greens so the added vitamin K makes the warfarin work harder."
"Leafy greens have no real effect on warfarin, so eat whatever amount you like."
questions.rat: Vitamin K is the antidote that opposes warfarin, so it is the day-to-day consistency of vitamin K intake — not avoidance — that keeps the INR stable; suddenly eliminating greens raises the warfarin effect just as suddenly bingeing lowers it. Cutting greens out or doubling them both swing the INR, and greens clearly do affect warfarin.
questions.strat: Warfarin + vitamin K = keep intake STEADY, not zero — big swings either way move the INR.

questions.stem: A client is being discharged on phenelzine, a monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI), for depression. The nurse teaches the client to avoid tyramine-containing foods to prevent a hypertensive crisis. Which foods should the nurse instruct the client to avoid? Select all that apply.
opts: Aged cheddar and blue cheese
Cured and smoked meats such as salami and pepperoni
Fermented foods such as sauerkraut and soy sauce
Fresh chicken breast cooked the same day
White rice and plain pasta
Tap water and decaffeinated herbal tea
questions.rat: Tyramine accumulates in aged, cured, smoked, and fermented foods; with an MAOI blocking its breakdown, tyramine drives vasoconstriction and a hypertensive crisis. Freshly cooked meats, plain grains, and water are low in tyramine and are safe.
questions.strat: Tyramine = AGED / cured / fermented. "If it's been sitting and ripening, skip it on an MAOI."

questions.stem: A client taking a statin reports drinking a large glass of grapefruit juice every morning with the medication. The nurse explains the interaction. Which statement best describes why this combination is a concern?
opts: Grapefruit blocks liver enzymes that break the drug down, so more drug stays active
Grapefruit speeds up the liver enzymes that break the drug down, so it is cleared too fast
Grapefruit binds the drug in the stomach and carries it out before much is absorbed
Grapefruit changes the drug's chemical structure so it gradually loses its effect
questions.rat: Grapefruit inhibits the cytochrome P450 enzymes that metabolize many drugs, so the drug is broken down more slowly, accumulates, and can reach toxic levels — with statins this raises the risk of muscle injury. It does not induce (speed up) metabolism, chelate the drug, or chemically inactivate it.
questions.strat: Grapefruit jams CYP450 → drug piles up → toxicity. Think "blocked enzyme = higher drug level."

questions.stem: A client is prescribed an oral tetracycline antibiotic and asks the nurse how to take it. The client routinely drinks a glass of milk and takes a calcium supplement at breakfast. Which instruction should the nurse give?
opts: Separate the antibiotic from milk and the calcium supplement by a couple of hours
Take the antibiotic together with the milk and calcium to protect the stomach lining
Take the calcium supplement at the exact same time so both are absorbed together
Stop the calcium supplement and avoid all dairy for the entire course of antibiotics
questions.rat: Tetracyclines (and fluoroquinolones) bind calcium in a chelation reaction that blocks the antibiotic's absorption, so the client should space the antibiotic and any calcium/dairy by a couple of hours rather than taking them together. Taking them together reduces absorption; stopping calcium entirely is unnecessary when timing alone solves the problem.
questions.strat: Calcium grabs the -cycline/-floxacin → separate the doses; you space them, you don't stop the calcium.

questions.stem: A nurse teaches a client newly prescribed levothyroxine how to take it. The client also takes a calcium and iron supplement. Which instruction reflects correct administration?
opts: Take it on an empty stomach before breakfast, apart from the calcium and iron
Take it with breakfast alongside the calcium and iron so the food prevents stomach upset
Take it at bedtime right after the calcium and iron, with a light snack to settle it
Take it whenever it is convenient, since meal timing has no effect on how it is absorbed
questions.rat: Levothyroxine absorption is unpredictable with food, so it is taken on an empty stomach 30–60 minutes before eating; calcium and iron bind it and must be separated by several hours. Taking it with food or alongside calcium/iron reduces absorption, and the timing very much matters.
questions.strat: Levothyroxine = empty stomach, before breakfast, away from calcium/iron.

questions.stem: The nurse reviews food and medication interactions with a client who takes several drugs. Match each medication to the correct teaching point the nurse should give.
shared: Take with food or milk to reduce stomach irritation
Take on an empty stomach, well before food, for reliable absorption
Keep day-to-day vitamin K (leafy green) intake consistent
Avoid grapefruit juice, which can raise the drug to toxic levels
questions.rat: Aspirin and NSAIDs irritate the gastric lining, so food or milk buffers them; levothyroxine needs an empty stomach for predictable absorption; warfarin's effect is opposed by vitamin K, so leafy-green intake should stay steady; grapefruit inhibits the enzymes that clear many statins, raising drug levels.
questions.strat: Sort by the mechanism: irritant→food, absorption-sensitive→empty stomach, antidote-food→keep steady, enzyme-blocker→avoid.

questions.stem: A 77-year-old client has taken digoxin with water for several months with good results. At a clinic visit a low calcium level is found, and a calcium supplement is added without a full medication review. Two weeks later the client returns to the emergency department with nausea, visual changes, and a slow, irregular pulse. Which conclusion best explains this client's presentation?
opts: Adding calcium without reviewing the interaction with digoxin contributed to digoxin toxicity
The calcium supplement blocked digoxin absorption, so the client is showing signs of an under-dose
The findings are unrelated to medications and reflect normal aging of the heart
The calcium corrected the deficiency, and these are the expected effects of a normal calcium level
questions.rat: The client shows classic digoxin toxicity (nausea, visual changes, bradydysrhythmia); calcium was added without a medication review that would have flagged its interaction with digoxin, so the missed interaction contributed to toxicity. The presentation is excess effect, not an under-dose, is clearly medication-related rather than normal aging, and these are not expected effects of a normal calcium level.
questions.strat: New drug + old drug + a sick patient = suspect the interaction; a skipped med review is the setup for toxicity.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a 30-year-old client who is 24 weeks pregnant about foods to avoid because of her higher risk for serious foodborne illness. The client lists several lunch options she enjoys. Which choice should the nurse advise her to avoid?
opts: A fresh apple she rinses well under cool running water before eating
A turkey sandwich layered with unpasteurized soft cheese
A cup of pasteurized plain yogurt topped with rinsed fresh berries
A firm hard-boiled egg she peels and eats with whole-grain crackers
questions.rat: Pregnant clients are a high-risk group; unpasteurized (raw-milk) soft cheese can carry Listeria, which crosses the placenta and threatens the fetus, so it should be avoided. Washed produce, pasteurized yogurt, and a fully cooked egg are safe and encouraged — they are distractors because clients may overgeneralize 'avoid dairy, fruit, and eggs.'
questions.strat: In pregnancy, the unpasteurized or raw item is the one to cut, not the cooked or pasteurized food.

questions.stem: A nurse reviewing food safety identifies a food most commonly linked to each foodborne organism. Match each organism to the food it is most often associated with in an outbreak.
questions.rat: Salmonella is classically tied to eggs, Campylobacter to raw poultry, norovirus and Vibrio to raw shellfish, and Listeria to unpasteurized milk and soft cheeses. The foods are similar 'raw/animal-source' choices, so the match rests on knowing each organism's signature high-risk food rather than guessing.
questions.strat: Anchor each bug to its signature food: Salmonella-eggs, Campylobacter-poultry, norovirus-shellfish, Listeria-raw dairy.

questions.stem: At a family picnic, a nurse notices a tray of cooked chicken has been sitting on a buffet table in the sun for about 3 hours at roughly 85 °F (29 °C). The host asks whether the chicken is still safe to eat. Which response by the nurse is best?
opts: "It is perfectly safe to eat, since it was thoroughly cooked all the way through earlier this same afternoon."
"Throw it out — food left over 2 hours in the danger zone grows bacteria."
"Just reheat it in the microwave for a minute or two and it will be perfectly safe to serve again."
"It is fine to keep serving as long as no one has noticed an off smell or any change in its color yet."
questions.rat: Bacteria multiply rapidly between 40 °F and 140 °F (the danger zone); perishable food held there longer than 2 hours should be discarded. Thorough earlier cooking does not protect food that later sits warm, reheating does not undo toxin buildup, and many pathogens produce no change in smell, color, or taste — these are common reasoning errors.
questions.strat: Time + temperature, not appearance: over 2 hours in the 40-140 °F danger zone means discard it.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a client the four steps to prevent foodborne illness — clean, separate, cook, and chill. Which client statements indicate correct understanding? Select all that apply.
opts: "I will refrigerate leftovers within 2 hours of cooking."
"I will use separate cutting boards for raw meat and for fresh produce."
"I will store raw chicken on the bottom shelf, below ready-to-eat foods."
"I will rinse the raw chicken under the faucet before I cook it."
"I will keep my refrigerator at or below 40 °F (4 °C)."
"I will reuse the marinade from the raw beef as a sauce without boiling it."
questions.rat: Refrigerating within 2 hours, using separate boards, storing raw meat below ready-to-eat food, and keeping the fridge at or below 40 °F all follow clean-separate-cook-chill. Rinsing raw poultry actually splashes bacteria around the sink and is not recommended, and reusing raw-meat marinade without boiling it transfers live pathogens onto cooked food — both are frequent, plausible mistakes.
questions.strat: Judge each statement on its own; the wrong ones spread germs (washing poultry, reusing raw marinade) rather than containing them.

questions.stem: A client phones the clinic 2 days after a suspected foodborne illness. Which report should the nurse identify as most concerning and warranting evaluation by a healthcare provider?
opts: Loose stools twice yesterday that have now slowed down
Bloody stools with a fever of 102.4 °F (39.1 °C)
Mild nausea relieved after sipping clear fluids
One episode of vomiting the first evening, none since
questions.rat: Bloody stools and a fever above 102 °F (38.9 °C) are warning signs of severe foodborne illness needing provider evaluation, as bloody diarrhea can signal infections such as E. coli that risk hemolytic uremic syndrome. Improving loose stools, nausea relieved by fluids, and a single early vomiting episode are mild, resolving symptoms — realistic distractors that mimic the concerning option in tone.
questions.strat: Among GI complaints, the bleeding-plus-high-fever combination is the red flag; resolving or mild symptoms are reassuring.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with coronary artery disease and newly diagnosed mild right-sided heart failure returns to clinic. Over the past month his weight is up 7 pounds, he has +2 pitting edema in both legs and ankles, and he reports fatigue. His lungs are clear and his appetite is unchanged. Which interpretation of these findings should most guide the nurse's plan of care?
opts: The weight gain reflects fluid retention from his heart failure
The weight gain shows his calorie intake finally meets his energy needs
The fatigue and weight gain point to early protein-calorie undernutrition
The clear lungs confirm the added weight is lean muscle gain from rest
questions.rat: A 7-pound gain over one month with new bilateral pitting edema in a client with right-sided heart failure is fluid retention, not nutritional improvement, so sodium and fluid management drive the plan. Reading it as adequate calorie intake or as muscle gain misattributes edema weight to nutrition, and undernutrition is unsupported since his appetite and intake are unchanged.
questions.strat: Rapid weight gain plus dependent edema equals fluid, not food - weigh the trend against the clinical picture before crediting it to intake.

questions.stem: A clinic nurse reviews four clients seen this morning. Which client should the nurse contact FIRST?
opts: A client on furosemide with new watery diarrhea, muscle cramps, and lightheadedness
A client with lactose intolerance asking which milk substitutes have the most calcium
A client newly started on a low-sodium diet who wants a printed list of foods to avoid
A client on the DASH plan asking whether nuts and seeds are allowed with each lunch
questions.rat: Diarrhea plus muscle cramps and lightheadedness in a client on a loop diuretic signals possible volume depletion and hypokalemia, an unstable physiologic problem that outranks the others. The lactose, low-sodium, and DASH questions are routine teaching needs that are important but stable and can wait.
questions.strat: Unstable physiologic findings (diuretic + cramps + dizziness = electrolyte/volume loss) beat routine teaching questions every time.

questions.stem: Two days after a family picnic, the 67-year-old client on furosemide calls the clinic. He has had worsening diarrhea since yesterday, a temperature of 100.8 degrees F (38 degrees C), and stomach cramps, and he says he feels weak and dizzy when he stands. Which action by the nurse is the priority?
opts: Advise him to be evaluated promptly for dehydration given the diarrhea and diuretic
Tell him to hold all oral fluids until the diarrhea resolves so his stomach can rest
Tell him to take one extra dose of his furosemide to clear the leftover excess fluid
Have him add a daily sports drink at home and recheck with the clinic in about one week
questions.rat: Diarrhea and fever in a client already taking a loop diuretic compound fluid and electrolyte loss, and orthostatic dizziness suggests he is becoming volume depleted, so prompt evaluation is the priority. Holding fluids worsens dehydration, an extra diuretic dose deepens the fluid and potassium loss, and waiting a week with only a sports drink delays care for a client who is already symptomatic.
questions.strat: When a diuretic and an acute GI illness stack fluid losses, escalate for evaluation - never withhold fluids or add diuretic.

questions.stem: An older adult who cannot meet needs orally is started on continuous enteral tube feedings. Which nursing actions support safe administration and monitoring? Select all that apply.
opts: Keep the head of the bed elevated at least 30 degrees during the feeding
Check gastric residual volume and tube placement per facility protocol
Monitor intake, output, daily weights, and electrolyte levels
Flush the tube with water before and after medications and per protocol
Hang each formula bag for 24 hours at room temperature to reduce waste
Give the ordered formula as a single rapid bolus to finish the feeding sooner
questions.rat: Elevating the head of the bed reduces aspiration, verifying placement and residuals confirms safe delivery, tracking I and O, weights, and electrolytes detects fluid and electrolyte shifts, and flushing keeps the tube patent. Hanging formula for a prolonged period at room temperature invites bacterial growth, and rapidly bolusing a continuous feeding causes cramping, diarrhea, and aspiration risk.
questions.strat: Enteral safety = head up, placement and residual checked, flush to keep patent, and watch fluid/electrolytes - never rush the rate or let formula sit out.

questions.stem: A client is receiving total parenteral nutrition through a central line. When the new bag is delayed and the current bag finishes early, which action by the nurse is most appropriate while waiting for pharmacy?
opts: Hang 10% dextrose at the same rate until the new TPN bag arrives
Stop the infusion and flush the line, then restart when the bag is ready
Slow the remaining TPN to a keep-open rate to make the bag last longer
Increase the next bag's rate later to make up the missed volume
questions.rat: Abruptly stopping TPN can cause rebound hypoglycemia, so hanging a dextrose solution at the same rate maintains the glucose load until the next bag is available. Stopping and flushing risks hypoglycemia, diluting or slowing the prescribed TPN alters the ordered nutrition, and doubling a later rate to catch up can cause hyperglycemia and fluid overload.
questions.strat: Never let TPN run dry or stop cold - bridge with dextrose at the same rate to prevent rebound hypoglycemia.

questions.stem: A nurse is helping a client who has dysphagia after a stroke begin a meal. The speech-language pathologist has set a modified-texture diet. Which nursing action best protects this client during eating?
opts: Seat the client fully upright and stay to monitor for choking
Let the client recline at 30 degrees to ease the workload of chewing
Offer thin liquids between bites to help wash food down
Encourage the client to eat quickly while assistance is available
questions.rat: Dysphagia carries a high risk of aspiration; the guide directs the nurse to have the client eat fully upright (aids swallowing), follow the SLP's texture orders, and monitor while eating in case of choking or aspiration. Reclining and rushing increase aspiration risk, and thin liquids are often the hardest consistency to control in dysphagia.
questions.strat: Dysphagia = airway first: upright, supervised, and only the texture the SLP approved.

questions.stem: A nurse is screening older adults in a clinic. Which findings or statements should prompt the nurse to evaluate further for dysphagia? Select all that apply.
opts: The client has Parkinson's disease
The client reports a recent stroke with residual weakness
The client says swallowing has gradually become harder over the past year
The client states, "I just figured trouble swallowing comes with getting old"
The client eats three balanced meals a day without coughing or choking
questions.rat: Neurologic conditions such as Parkinson's disease and stroke, and a narrowing or weakening of the swallowing structures with age, are risk factors the guide names for dysphagia, so each warrants further assessment. The belief that swallowing trouble is a normal part of aging is itself a teaching opportunity, because the guide states dysphagia is NOT a normal part of aging. A client eating full meals with no coughing or choking shows no current swallowing difficulty.
questions.strat: Risk factors and a 'normal aging' myth both flag for follow-up; an asymptomatic eater does not.

questions.stem: A client with bothersome intestinal gas and flatulence is starting an elimination diet after the provider ruled out a medical cause. Using the guide's list of gas-producing foods, classify each item as one to limit on a gas-reduction plan or one less likely to trigger gas.
questions.rat: The guide's 'Foods That Contribute to Gas Production' list includes legumes (beans, peas), cruciferous and certain other vegetables (cabbage, broccoli, cauliflower), milk and milk products, carbonated beverages, and fatty/fried foods. Lean grilled chicken and plain white rice are not on the gas-producing list, so they are reasonable choices on a gas-reduction plan.
questions.strat: Beans, brassicas, dairy, and carbonation drive gas; plain lean protein and white rice are gentler.

questions.stem: A client reports frequent belching and a sensation of trapped stomach gas. Which client habit should the nurse address first as a contributor to swallowed air?
opts: Drinking carbonated soft drinks and chewing gum often
Eating a serving of whole-grain wheat bread with every lunch
Taking a relaxed 20-minute walk after the evening meal each night
Having a small bowl of plain oatmeal at breakfast most mornings
questions.rat: The guide attributes stomach gas to habits that let air enter and become trapped: carbonated drinks, eating too fast, smoking, chewing gum, and sucking on hard candy. Whole-grain bread is on the intestinal gas list rather than the swallowed-air list, and walking after a meal and plain oatmeal are not identified as causes of stomach gas.
questions.strat: Swallowed-air gas = carbonation, gum, fast eating, candy, smoking.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching about anti-inflammatory eating patterns recommended for general GI health. The [1] uses olive oil as its primary fat with plenty of fruits, vegetables, and grains, while the [2] emphasizes minerals such as potassium and is built around limiting [3].

blanks.label: [1] Diet described
opts: Mediterranean diet
ketogenic diet
low-residue diet

blanks.label: [2] Second diet
opts: DASH diet
clear liquid diet
high-protein diet

blanks.label: [3] DASH limits
opts: dietary fiber
sodium
potassium

questions.rat: The guide identifies the Mediterranean diet (olive oil as primary fat, abundant produce and grains) and the DASH diet as the two anti-inflammatory patterns recommended for GI health. DASH emphasizes minerals such as potassium, calcium, and magnesium and works by decreasing sodium, not fiber or potassium.
questions.strat: Mediterranean = olive oil + produce; DASH = up the minerals, down the sodium.

questions.stem: A nurse reviews orders for a client who needs a low-residue (low-fiber) diet for a GI disorder and notices a prior recommendation to follow the DASH eating plan. What does the nurse recognize about combining these?
opts: The DASH plan conflicts here, because it is high in fiber while a low-residue diet is needed
The DASH plan is ideal here, because its lower sodium content also keeps the fiber intake low
The two plans are essentially interchangeable, since both are meant to reduce GI inflammation
The DASH plan should replace the low-residue order to give better control of GI inflammation
questions.rat: The guide cautions that DASH is not appropriate for every GI disorder, specifically those requiring a low-residue (low-fiber) diet, because the DASH plan is built around a high fiber intake (about 30 g daily). Low sodium does not lower fiber, the two plans are not interchangeable, and a fiber-rich plan should not override a low-residue order.
questions.strat: DASH is high-fiber, so it clashes with any low-residue order.

questions.stem: A nurse uses a 24-hour dietary recall to screen a newly admitted client's intake. Which limitations of this method should the nurse keep in mind when interpreting the results? Select all that apply.
opts: The client may not recall the type or amount of food eaten
The last 24 hours may not reflect the client's usual intake
Snacks, sauces, and condiments are often underreported
Beverages may be left out unless the client is specifically prompted
The tool requires a multi-day inpatient stay before it can be completed
questions.rat: The guide lists these drawbacks of the 24-hour recall: the person may not recall the type or amount eaten, a single day may be atypical, snacks/gravies/sauces/condiments are commonly underreported, and liquids are omitted unless the client is prompted. The recall covers only the prior day and does not require a multi-day stay, so that option is not a limitation.
questions.strat: 24-hour recall is quick but leaky: memory, an atypical day, forgotten extras, and missed drinks.

questions.stem:
A client with dysphagia is ordered an IDDSI Level 4 (extremely thick / pureed) diet with IDDSI Level 2 (mildly thick) liquids. The dietary tray arrives and the nurse checks each item against the prescribed consistencies before the client eats.

For each item, indicate whether it is appropriate to serve or should be withheld for this prescription.

rows.t: Instant pudding (holds its shape on a spoon)
Pureed meat that cannot be poured
A nectar-thick milkshake sippable through a straw with effort
A glass of plain water (thin liquid)
A whole hamburger on a bun
Vegetable broth that flows like water

questions.rat: Level 4 foods are pureed/extremely thick — they hold their shape and cannot be poured (instant pudding, pureed meat), so they match the order. Level 2 mildly thick liquids flow off a spoon and are sippable through a straw with effort (the nectar-thick milkshake), so that liquid is correct. Thin liquids that flow like water (plain water, vegetable broth) are Level 0 and are unsafe for a client needing Level 2 thickening, and a hamburger on a bun is regular/Level 7 texture far beyond a Level 4 pureed order.
questions.strat: Match the tray to the ordered IDDSI level: pureed holds its shape, mildly thick liquids flow off a spoon — anything that flows like water is too thin.

questions.stem: A nurse is assisting a client with dysphagia at mealtime and is reinforcing measures to prevent aspiration. Which actions should the nurse take? Select all that apply.
opts: Position the client upright with the chin tucked toward the chest while eating
Offer small bites of about 1 teaspoon and allow 20 to 30 minutes for the meal
Keep the client sitting upright for a period of time after the meal is finished
Encourage the client to talk and socialize during each bite to stay relaxed
Have the client sip liquids to wash down each mouthful of solid food
Hold the chin up and the head tilted back to help the food slide down
questions.rat: Upright positioning with a chin tuck closes the airway and directs the bolus safely, small bites with unhurried time reduce the volume that can be aspirated, and staying upright after eating limits reflux and aspiration. Talking while eating opens the airway and invites aspiration; using liquids to wash down food moves it past an unprepared swallow (liquids only after the mouth is cleared); and tilting the head back opens the airway, the opposite of the protective chin-tuck.
questions.strat: Aspiration precautions: sit up, chin DOWN, small slow bites, stay up afterward — never talk through a bite, wash food down, or tip the head back.

questions.stem:
CASE (1/6) Recognize cues. A 71-year-old client is three days post ischemic stroke and is eating a regular diet with thin liquids. The nurse observes the client during lunch and reviews the record.

Which findings are relevant cues of a swallowing problem? Select all that apply.
opts: Coughing and throat clearing each time the client sips thin liquids
A wet, gurgly voice quality after swallowing
Chewed food pocketed in the cheek on the affected side
Unintentional loss of 3 kg over the past two weeks
Blood pressure 132/78 mm Hg
Temperature 37.0 degrees C
questions.rat: Coughing or choking with thin liquids, a wet or gurgly voice (food resting on the vocal cords), pocketing food in the cheek, and unintended weight loss are classic manifestations of impaired swallowing and reduced intake after a stroke. The normal blood pressure and temperature are not cues of a swallowing disorder.
questions.strat: Recognize cues: pull the swallowing red flags — cough with liquids, wet voice, pocketing, weight loss — and leave the normal vitals.

questions.stem:
CASE (2/6) Analyze cues. The same client coughs on thin liquids, has a wet voice after swallowing, pockets food, and has lost weight. The stroke has weakened the muscles involved in swallowing.

How should the nurse interpret these findings?
opts: Normal age-related slowing of swallowing that requires no dietary change
Impaired swallowing from the stroke, placing the client at aspiration risk
Simple loss of appetite that should resolve once the client is less fatigued
An adverse effect of the stroke medications rather than a swallowing problem
questions.rat: Coughing on liquids, a wet voice, and pocketing together point to neuromuscular dysphagia from the stroke (impaired oral and pharyngeal control), which lets food and liquid enter the airway and creates aspiration risk. These are not normal aging, simple poor appetite, or a medication effect — the cluster of swallowing-specific findings names the problem.
questions.strat: Cluster the cues: cough on liquids + wet voice + pocketing after a stroke = impaired swallow with aspiration risk.

questions.stem:
CASE (3/6) Prioritize hypotheses. The nurse confirms the client has stroke-related dysphagia and is aspirating thin liquids.

Which risk is the priority concern for this client?
opts: Aspiration of food or liquid causing airway compromise and pneumonia
Constipation from reduced dietary fiber while on a texture-modified diet
Pressure injury from sitting upright in a chair throughout each meal
Boredom and social isolation related to the newly restricted diet
questions.rat: Aspiration is the priority because food or liquid entering the airway threatens breathing and can cause aspiration pneumonia — an airway/breathing problem outranks the others by the ABC framework. Constipation, pressure injury, and social isolation are real concerns with dysphagia but none is immediately life-threatening.
questions.strat: Airway beats everything: aspiration is the danger that can kill, so it is the priority hypothesis.

questions.stem: CASE (4/6) Generate solutions (bowtie). Choose the central problem, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor for this client with stroke-related dysphagia.

opts: Risk for aspiration related to impaired swallowing
Risk for fluid overload related to thickened liquids
Risk for impaired skin integrity related to immobility
Provide thickened liquids and a texture-modified diet per the swallow evaluation
Position the client upright and supervise feeding at every meal
Resume thin liquids to keep the client well hydrated
Feed the client quickly so the tray does not get cold
Lung sounds, oxygen saturation, and signs of coughing or choking
Daily weight and amount of food and fluid taken
Pupil size and reaction to light
Bowel sounds in all four quadrants

questions.rat: The central problem is risk for aspiration from impaired swallowing. The correct actions are to modify textures and thicken liquids as the swallow evaluation directs and to keep the client upright with supervised feeding — both reduce aspiration. Resuming thin liquids or rushing the meal would increase aspiration. The parameters that track this problem are respiratory status (lung sounds, SpO2, coughing/choking) and nutritional adequacy (weight and intake); pupils and bowel sounds do not monitor aspiration.
questions.strat: Aspiration plan: thicken and modify, sit up and supervise, then watch the lungs/SpO2 and the weight/intake.

questions.stem: CASE (5/6) Take action. The plan of care for aspiration risk is started. Which nursing actions are appropriate to implement for this client? Select all that apply.
opts: Place the client in a high-Fowler's position before beginning the meal
Obtain a swallow evaluation and speech-language pathology referral
Provide oral care before and after each of the client's meals
Keep the client sitting upright for at least 30 minutes after eating
Cue the client to take small bites and dry-swallow between mouthfuls
Encourage the client to recline after meals to ease digestive effort
questions.rat: The first five are aspiration precautions grounded in dysphagia care: high-Fowler's positioning protects the airway, a swallow evaluation/SLP referral individualizes the diet and liquid consistency, oral care reduces oral bacteria and aspiration-pneumonia risk and improves taste, staying upright after meals limits reflux and aspiration, and small bites with dry swallows clear residue before the next bite. Reclining after a meal promotes reflux and aspiration and is the one action to avoid.
questions.strat: Take action = the upright aspiration bundle; the only trap is letting the client lie back after eating.

questions.stem:
CASE (6/6) Evaluate outcomes. After several days on thickened liquids, a texture-modified diet, and supervised upright feeding, the nurse evaluates the client's response.

Which finding best indicates that the plan of care is working?
opts: The client eats without coughing, the lungs are clear, and intake is maintained
The client finishes meals faster now that thin liquids have been returned to the tray
The client coughs only occasionally on liquids but has lost another kilogram of weight
The lung sounds are clear, although the client is now eating only half of each meal
questions.rat: Success is the absence of coughing or choking with meals, clear lung sounds, and maintained food and fluid intake — the airway is protected and nutrition is preserved. Adding thin liquids back is not an improvement and reintroduces aspiration risk; occasional coughing with continued weight loss shows ongoing aspiration and inadequate intake; and clear lungs with only half the meal eaten means nutrition is not being maintained.
questions.strat: It worked when there is no coughing, the lungs stay clear, and intake holds — partial wins that lose weight or add thin liquids are not success.

questions.stem: A client in the acute phase of diarrhea (loose, watery stools for the past 2 days) asks the nurse what they should eat today. Which meal should the nurse recommend?
opts: A bran muffin with a glass of cold milk
Banana slices with white rice and dry toast
A green salad with raw vegetables and beans
Popcorn with an apple and a glass of apple juice
questions.rat: During the acute phase, low-fiber foods such as bananas, rice, applesauce, and dry toast are recommended because they are easily tolerated. Bran, raw fruits/vegetables, beans, popcorn, and milk products are all to be limited: high fiber and gas-forming foods worsen diarrhea, milk's lactose may not be digested, and apple juice can aggravate diarrhea.
questions.strat: Acute diarrhea = low-fiber, bland (BRAT-style); cut milk, raw produce, and high-fiber foods.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old client has had watery diarrhea for 3 days. Which finding most strongly indicates a complication that the nurse should report to the provider?
opts: Mild abdominal cramping that returns with each loose stool
A sudden urgent need to get to the bathroom for a stool
Concentrated urine, new confusion, and a rapid heart rate
Bloating and nausea that ease after passing the stool
questions.rat: Dark concentrated urine, confusion, and tachycardia are reported manifestations of dehydration, a dangerous complication of diarrhea that is especially likely in older adults. Cramping, urgency, bloating, and nausea are common manifestations of diarrhea itself, not signs of a complication requiring a report.
questions.strat: Sort routine diarrhea symptoms from dehydration red flags (urine, mental status, heart rate) — older adults decompensate fast.

questions.stem: The nurse is teaching about the two types of dietary fiber. The type of fiber that dissolves in water, absorbs water during digestion, and is used in bulk-forming laxatives is [1] fiber. The type that does not dissolve, stays unchanged during digestion, and promotes normal movement of intestinal contents is [2] fiber, found in foods such as [3].

blanks.label: [1] Dissolves and absorbs water
opts: water-soluble
water-insoluble

blanks.label: [2] Promotes movement of contents
opts: water-soluble
water-insoluble

blanks.label: [3] Source of that fiber
opts: oats and oat bran
whole-wheat bread and brown rice
apples and oranges

questions.rat: Water-soluble fiber dissolves and absorbs water during digestion, increases stool bulk, may lower cholesterol, and is the basis of bulk-forming laxatives (oats, oat bran, beans, citrus). Water-insoluble fiber stays unchanged and promotes normal movement of intestinal contents; it is found in whole-grain products such as whole-wheat bread and brown rice. Oats/oat bran and apples/oranges are soluble-fiber sources, making them wrong for the insoluble blank.
questions.strat: Soluble = dissolves + bulks (laxative base); insoluble = unchanged + moves things along (whole grains/bran).

questions.stem: A client with chronic constipation who eats a low-fiber diet asks how to manage it without relying on medication. Which instructions should the nurse include? Select all that apply.
opts: Gradually increase fiber from fruits, vegetables, and whole grains
Drink about 8 to 10 glasses of water each day
Walk and become more physically active
Hold bowel movements until a scheduled time each day
Skip meals to give the bowel a rest between movements
Establish a regular eating routine and respond to the body's cues
questions.rat: Care for constipation centers on increasing fiber, increasing water (8-10 glasses/day), increasing activity, keeping a regular eating routine, and listening to the body's cues for bowel movements. Ignoring or delaying the urge works against those cues, and skipping meals is specifically discouraged because a regular eating routine should be maintained.
questions.strat: Constipation triad = more fiber + more fluid + more movement; honor the urge, don't suppress it or skip meals.

questions.stem: A client recovering from several days of diarrhea is rebuilding fluid and electrolyte intake. Which statement indicates the client correctly understood the nurse's teaching?
opts: "A prescribed electrolyte drink replaces the sodium and potassium I lost."
"Warm milk taken between my meals is a gentle way to rebuild my strength."
"Drinking apple juice with each meal keeps me hydrated and adds potassium."
"I should drink large amounts of fluid during meals to help dilute the food."
questions.rat: Electrolyte solutions such as Pedialyte or Gatorade may be prescribed to replenish lost sodium and potassium, the priority during recovery from diarrhea. Milk products are avoided because lactose may not be digested, apple juice can aggravate diarrhea, and liquids should be taken 30 minutes before or after meals rather than during them.
questions.strat: Replace what diarrhea drains (sodium/potassium) with a prescribed electrolyte solution; separate fluids from meals and skip milk and apple juice.

questions.stem: A clinic nurse is following up four clients with gastrointestinal complaints. Which client should the nurse assess FIRST?
opts: An older adult with two days of vomiting and watery diarrhea who is dizzy on standing
A client with chronic constipation asking how much water to drink each day to stay regular
A client with a new colostomy who wants help planning meals before going out in public
A client with occasional heartburn asking which foods seem to trigger the discomfort
questions.rat: Two days of vomiting and diarrhea with dizziness on standing point to fluid volume deficit, an unstable physiologic problem that outranks the others. The constipation, colostomy, and heartburn clients have real but stable teaching and coping needs that can wait.
questions.strat: Fluid losses plus orthostatic dizziness signal volume deficit - the unstable physiologic client is seen before routine teaching needs.

questions.stem: A client recovering from an acute episode of diarrhea is started on a BRAT diet as symptoms settle. Which meal selection by the client tells the nurse the teaching about this diet was understood?
opts: Banana, white rice, applesauce, and plain toast
Bran cereal, raw spinach salad, and fresh orange slices
Fried chicken, buttered corn, and a glass of whole milk
Black bean chili with brown rice and a side of broccoli
questions.rat: The BRAT diet is bananas, rice, applesauce, and toast - low-fiber, bland foods that are gentle on irritated GI mucosa. High-fiber choices like bran, raw vegetables, beans, and broccoli, or rich fried and high-fat foods, can further irritate the gut and worsen symptoms.
questions.strat: BRAT spells the menu - bananas, rice, applesauce, toast - low-fiber and bland, not high-fiber or greasy.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing a focused nutritional assessment for a client reporting new gastrointestinal symptoms. Which assessment actions are appropriate for recognizing cues in this client? Select all that apply.
opts: Ask which foods seem to trigger or relieve the reported symptoms
Use the PQRST mnemonic to evaluate any reported abdominal pain
Establish the client's usual bowel pattern and ask about recent changes
Ask about cultural and religious influences on the client's dietary intake
Advise the client to begin a high-fiber diet before the cause is identified
questions.rat: A focused GI assessment identifies trigger and relieving foods, evaluates abdominal pain with PQRST, establishes the baseline bowel pattern to detect changes, and explores cultural and religious dietary influences. Starting a high-fiber diet is a planned intervention, not an assessment, and prescribing it before the cause is known could worsen an irritated gut.
questions.strat: Recognizing cues means gathering data first - trigger foods, PQRST pain, bowel baseline, culture - not jumping to an intervention.

questions.stem: A client with several days of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea has dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output, and a blood pressure that drops when standing. Several nursing diagnoses apply. Which one should the nurse address as the highest priority?
opts: Fluid volume deficit related to vomiting and diarrhea
Imbalanced nutrition: less than body requirements
Ineffective individual coping related to the recent illness
Fatigue related to the current nutritional deficits
questions.rat: Dry mucous membranes, low urine output, and orthostatic blood pressure changes indicate fluid volume deficit, a physiologic threat that takes priority over the nutrition, coping, and fatigue diagnoses. The other diagnoses are valid but less urgent once hydration is stabilized.
questions.strat: Rank hypotheses by risk - active fluid loss with orthostatic changes is the most urgent, so volume deficit comes before nutrition or coping.

questions.stem: A client recovering from an episode of biliary colic asks the nurse which meals are least likely to trigger another attack. Which menu choice should the nurse recommend?
opts: Fried chicken sandwich with a buttered roll and a milkshake
Grilled white fish with steamed vegetables and brown rice
Pasta with cream sauce and a slice of cheesecake
Cheeseburger with french fries and a soda
questions.rat: Biliary colic is triggered by meals high in calories, fat, and carbohydrates; a low-fat, low-carbohydrate meal such as grilled fish with vegetables is best. The fried, creamy, and high-fat/high-carb choices each provoke gallbladder contraction and pain.
questions.strat: Gallbladder pain follows fat and big carb loads — pick the leanest plate.

questions.stem: A client is admitted with gallstone pancreatitis and reports upper abdominal pain radiating to the back that worsens after eating. Which dietary order does the nurse anticipate during the initial inflammatory phase?
opts: Withhold all food and fluids and infuse intravenous fluids
Start a high-protein, high-calorie oral diet right away
Offer small, frequent low-fat oral meals as the client tolerates them
Provide a full clear-liquid diet and advance as tolerated
questions.rat: In gallstone pancreatitis the client avoids food and fluids until the inflammation subsides while IV fluids are given; IV nutrients are added only if pancreatitis is severe and inflammation continues. Oral diets of any kind during the acute phase stimulate the inflamed pancreas.
questions.strat: Acute pancreatitis = rest the pancreas: NPO with IV fluids first.

questions.stem: The nurse is teaching a client with several risk factors for gallstones about diet choices that may lower the risk of stone formation. Which statements by the nurse are appropriate? Select all that apply.
opts: "Build your meals around plenty of fruits and vegetables."
"Include whole-grain foods in your diet."
"Choose a diet that is high in fat and low in fiber."
"Limiting fatty foods can help reduce your risk."
"Increasing fiber in your diet is helpful."
questions.rat: Diets high in fat and low in fiber increase gallstone risk, so the protective teaching is a diet high in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and fiber while limiting fat. Recommending a high-fat, low-fiber diet does the opposite and would raise the risk.
questions.strat: Lower gallstone risk = more fiber and produce, less fat — flag the option that says the reverse.

questions.stem: A client newly diagnosed with viral hepatitis asks the nurse what dietary changes are needed. The client has no other GI complications. Which response is most accurate?
opts: "Usually no special diet is needed; eat balanced meals and avoid alcohol."
"You will need a strict low-protein diet to protect and rest your liver."
"A gluten-free diet is required to reduce the inflammation in your liver."
"Follow a high-fat diet so your liver has extra energy to repair itself."
questions.rat: Clients with hepatitis usually do not require a special diet; recommendations are regular balanced meals, adequate protein, limiting fatty/salty/sugary foods, and avoiding alcohol. Strict protein restriction, gluten-free, and high-fat instructions are not indicated by this material.
questions.strat: Hepatitis without complications = no special diet; the constants are balanced eating and no alcohol.

questions.stem: A client with obesity and metabolic syndrome is diagnosed with nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD). Which dietary plan best supports reversing the fatty infiltration of the liver?
opts: A calorie-controlled diet limiting fats, fructose, and alcohol
A high-protein diet with no limit placed on total daily calories
A liberal-fat diet that avoids all carbohydrates and fructose
An alcohol-only restriction while keeping all other intake the same
questions.rat: NAFLD is managed with a calorie-controlled, well-balanced diet that limits fats, fructose, and alcohol, along with tracking glucose and lipids, to reverse fatty infiltration. Unrestricted calories, a liberal-fat plan, or restricting alcohol alone do not address the excess fat deposits driving the disease.
questions.strat: NAFLD targets the fat in the liver — control calories, cut fat, fructose, and alcohol together.

questions.stem: A client with celiac disease who has not been following the prescribed diet reports right upper quadrant discomfort after fatty meals. The nurse understands that intestinal damage in celiac disease raises gallbladder risk through which mechanism?
opts: Reduced cholecystokinin production delays gallbladder emptying
Excess cholecystokinin causes the gallbladder to empty too quickly
Increased bile production overwhelms the gallbladder
Direct autoimmune destruction of the gallbladder wall
questions.rat: In celiac disease the damaged small-intestine lining limits cholecystokinin production, which delays gallbladder emptying and increases gallbladder problems, especially after a fatty meal. The other options reverse the hormone effect or describe mechanisms not supported by the material.
questions.strat: Damaged gut = less CCK = sluggish gallbladder emptying after fatty food.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman is admitted with cholecystitis and confirmed gallstones while awaiting cholecystectomy. She rates her right upper quadrant pain 10 out of 10 and has been vomiting repeatedly for the past day. The nurse reviews the nursing diagnoses that apply. Which problem should the nurse gather more data on to settle as the highest priority?
opts: Whether vomiting has produced a fluid and electrolyte imbalance
Knowledge deficit about the dietary changes she will need after surgery
Risk for infection related to the planned surgical incision
Anxiety related to the upcoming gallbladder removal
questions.rat: When a client has both severe pain and enough vomiting to threaten fluid and electrolyte balance, both are high priority and the nurse needs more data on the volume status to rank them, since an actual physiologic imbalance can outrank even severe pain. Knowledge deficit, risk for infection, and anxiety are valid but lower-acuity diagnoses that do not address an immediate physiologic threat.
questions.strat: When two physiologic problems compete, gather data on the one that can destabilize the client - active fluid loss - before settling the priority.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing a client with suspected gallbladder disease. For each finding, indicate whether it is consistent with biliary obstruction or is an unrelated finding.

rows.t: Right upper quadrant pain that worsens after fried food
Yellowing of the skin and sclera
Bright red blood coating formed stool

questions.rat: Obstruction of the bile ducts backs bile up, producing pale or chalky stools, dark urine, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain that worsens after fatty or fried meals. Bright red blood coating the stool reflects lower GI or rectal bleeding, not biliary obstruction.
questions.strat: Biliary obstruction blocks bile flow - stool loses color, urine darkens, skin yellows; frank red rectal bleeding points elsewhere.

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a client about eating habits in the first weeks after a cholecystectomy to limit diarrhea, gas, and bloating. Which instructions should the nurse include? Select all that apply.
opts: Choose foods labeled with no more than 3 grams of fat per serving
Eat smaller, more frequent meals rather than a few large ones
Increase soluble fiber such as oats and barley gradually over several weeks
Avoid fried, greasy foods and fatty sauces for at least the first week
Add a large amount of fiber right away to firm up the stools quickly
Plan meals around lean protein, vegetables, fruits, and whole grains
questions.rat: Low-fat choices (no more than 3 g fat per serving), smaller frequent meals that mix better with the now continuous bile, a gradual increase in soluble fiber, avoiding fried and greasy foods for at least a week, and meals built on lean protein, produce, and whole grains all reduce post-surgical diarrhea, gas, and bloating. Adding a large amount of fiber all at once worsens gas and cramping, so fiber must be increased slowly.
questions.strat: After gallbladder removal, less fat per meal and slow fiber buildup - the body can no longer store concentrated bile for a big fatty load.

questions.stem: Ten days after a cholecystectomy, a client returns reporting gas, bloating, and diarrhea. Her surgical pain is gone, but she says she still loves hamburgers and french fries and has only cut back on fast-food visits. Which explanation should the nurse use to help her connect her symptoms to her diet?
opts: Bile now drains continuously and is less concentrated, so a large fat load in one meal stays undigested and causes diarrhea
The diarrhea most likely means surgery did not fully clear the stones, so she should be re-evaluated for retained gallstones
These symptoms are an expected wound infection after surgery, so she should watch closely for incision redness and fever
The oats and barley she added for fiber are irritating her gut, so she should stop eating high-fiber foods entirely
questions.rat: After cholecystectomy, bile is less concentrated and drains continuously into the intestine, so a large fat load in one meal is not fully digested and produces gas, bloating, and a laxative effect - which fits her continued high-fat fast-food intake. Retained stones and wound infection do not explain diet-triggered symptoms with resolved pain, and soluble fiber such as oats and barley helps normalize bowel movements rather than causing the problem.
questions.strat: Match the symptom to the new physiology - continuous, dilute bile cannot handle a big fat load, so the fatty meals, not retained stones, drive the diarrhea.

questions.stem:
A nurse is reinforcing teaching about carbohydrate counting for a client with diabetes who uses mealtime insulin. The client asks, "Why does my dietitian keep talking about counting carbs?"

Which response by the nurse best reflects the purpose of carbohydrate counting?
opts: "It balances the carbohydrate you eat with the insulin you need for glucose control."
"It removes carbohydrate from your meals so that your blood glucose will stay low through the day."
"It locks you into the very same fixed gram amount of carbohydrate at every meal and every snack."
"It takes the place of seeing the dietitian once you have learned how to read the food labels."
questions.rat: Carbohydrate counting establishes a balance between the amount of carbohydrate eaten and the insulin needed to regulate blood glucose, and it is a flexible strategy that helps the client see how food choices affect glucose. It does not eliminate carbohydrate (carbs from fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, and low-fat milk are encouraged), is flexible rather than a fixed gram count, and the registered dietitian remains an essential part of the team.
questions.strat: Counting carbs matches carb intake to insulin — it is a flexible balancing tool, not carb elimination.

questions.stem:
A client with diabetes is learning to choose lower–glycemic-index foods to blunt post-meal glucose spikes, because high–glycemic-index foods raise blood sugar higher and faster. The client is deciding between four breakfast options.

Which choice has the LOWEST glycemic index?
opts: Oatmeal with an apple
Cornflakes with a sliced banana
White wheat toast with a glass of watermelon juice
A baguette with boiled potatoes
questions.rat: Oatmeal, beans, and apples fall in the low (30–50) glycemic-index range, so oatmeal with an apple raises glucose the slowest. Cornflakes and watermelon sit at 70–100, white wheat bread and boiled potatoes are also 70–100, and a banana is a mid-range (50–70) food — all raise glucose higher and faster than the oatmeal-and-apple option.
questions.strat: Whole grains and most whole fruits (oats, apples) run low-GI; refined grains, potatoes, cornflakes, and watermelon run high-GI.

questions.stem: A nurse is reinforcing teaching for a client with type 2 diabetes about alcohol use and managing a minor illness at home. Which statements by the client require further teaching because they are incorrect? Select all that apply.
opts: "While I'm sick I should stop eating completely until I start to feel better again."
"If I'm sick I should drink only regular soda and juice to keep my strength up."
"If I drink alcohol, I should have it together with food so that I don't go too low."
"When I am sick I should check my blood glucose more often than I usually do."
"When I am sick I should keep on taking my usual diabetes medications."
"I should call my provider for moderate ketones or a glucose reading over 250."
questions.rat: The two incorrect statements are stopping all food (the client should stick to the normal meal plan, not stop eating) and drinking only regular soda and juice (sick-day fluids should be sugar-free, such as water and tea). The other statements are correct sick-day and alcohol guidance: take alcohol with food to prevent hypoglycemia, check glucose more often, continue usual medications, and call the provider for moderate ketones or glucose over 250 mg/dL.
questions.strat: Sick-day rules: keep meds + normal meals, more frequent checks, sugar-free fluids, call for ketones / glucose >250.

questions.stem:
CASE (1/6) Recognize cues. A 24-year-old client with type 1 diabetes comes to clinic. The client reports feeling "shaky and sweaty" before lunch most days and has been skipping the bedtime snack and eating irregularly. Vital signs are within normal limits. Findings on review:

- States meals are eaten "whenever there's time," often hours apart
- Reports recent shakiness, nervousness, and sweating relieved by drinking juice
- A1C has risen since the last visit with no clear explanation
- Has been exercising more than usual to "keep weight off"
- Reports occasional missed mealtime insulin doses
- Takes a daily multivitamin

Which findings are relevant cues the nurse should follow up on? Select all that apply.
opts: Episodes of shakiness, nervousness, and sweating relieved by juice
Irregular meals eaten hours apart with a skipped bedtime snack
Unexplained rise in A1C
Increased exercise to keep weight off
Occasional missed mealtime insulin doses
Taking a daily multivitamin
questions.rat: Shakiness, nervousness, and sweating relieved by carbohydrate are classic hypoglycemia signs; irregular meals and a skipped snack set the client up for lows; and an unexplained A1C rise, excessive exercise to control weight, and omitted insulin are recognized warning signs of disordered eating in type 1 diabetes. A daily multivitamin is an expected, non-concerning finding and is not a relevant cue.
questions.strat: Cluster the abnormal/at-risk findings; a routine multivitamin is normal and is not a cue to chase.

questions.stem:
CASE (2/6) Analyze cues. The nurse considers the cluster of cues: pre-lunch shakiness, nervousness, and sweating relieved by juice, occurring with irregular meals and a skipped bedtime snack in a client on insulin.

Which explanation best accounts for these findings?
opts: Recurrent hypoglycemia from insulin paired with inconsistent food intake
Hyperglycemia caused by eating far too much carbohydrate at each of the meals
Early diabetic ketoacidosis brought on by the recently missed mealtime insulin doses
An expected and harmless response to the recent increase in physical activity
questions.rat: Hunger, nervousness, shakiness, and sweating that resolve with carbohydrate point to hypoglycemia (blood glucose under 70 mg/dL), which fits insulin use paired with irregular, skipped meals. Hyperglycemia produces thirst, fatigue, frequent urination, and blurred vision rather than relief from juice; DKA is a life-threatening crisis with Kussmaul respirations, not transient symptoms relieved by a drink; and these symptoms are not a normal, expected response to exercise.
questions.strat: Symptoms relieved by sugar = the body was low; match the symptom set to hypo- vs. hyperglycemia.

questions.stem:
CASE (3/6) Prioritize hypotheses. The client is alert and able to swallow but reports another wave of shakiness and sweating right now in the clinic. The nurse identifies several issues to address: recurrent hypoglycemia, irregular meal timing, missed insulin doses, and possible disordered eating.

Which problem should the nurse address FIRST?
opts: Treat the current hypoglycemic episode
Arrange a mental health referral for disordered eating
Reinforce teaching about consistent meal timing
Review the client's insulin-dosing technique
questions.rat: An active hypoglycemic episode is the acute, physiologic priority — untreated hypoglycemia can progress to unconsciousness — so the nurse treats the low first (Maslow/airway-circulation, acute over chronic). The mental health referral, meal-timing teaching, and insulin review are all appropriate but address ongoing/chronic issues that follow once the client is stabilized.
questions.strat: Treat the acute physiologic emergency now; teaching and referrals come after the client is safe.

questions.stem: CASE (4/6) Generate solutions (bowtie). The alert, awake client is currently hypoglycemic with shakiness and sweating and is able to swallow. Choose the central problem, two immediate actions, and two parameters to monitor — all grounded in the guide.

opts: Hypoglycemia in a client able to swallow
Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar nonketotic syndrome
Diabetic ketoacidosis
Give a carbohydrate-rich food such as a glucose tablet or juice
Stay with the client and reassess symptoms
Administer an injection of glucagon now
Withhold all food until symptoms resolve
Blood glucose level
Symptoms such as shakiness, sweating, and confusion
Urine ketones
Daily multivitamin intake

questions.rat: For a conscious client who can swallow, hypoglycemia is treated by consuming a carbohydrate-rich food (glucose tablet, juice, or honey); glucagon is reserved for the client who is unconscious or unable to swallow, so it is not the action here, and withholding food would worsen the low. Monitoring blood glucose and the hypoglycemia symptom set (shakiness, sweating, confusion) tells the nurse whether treatment worked; urine ketones belong to sick-day/DKA monitoring and multivitamin intake is irrelevant to this episode.
questions.strat: Conscious + can swallow = oral carbs and recheck; glucagon is only for the can't-swallow client.

questions.stem: CASE (5/6) Take action. The client's episode is resolving and the nurse develops a plan to prevent future lows and support overall self-management. Which actions should the nurse include? Select all that apply.
opts: Reinforce eating meals on a consistent schedule rather than hours apart
Initiate a referral to a registered dietitian for an individualized meal plan
Initiate a mental health referral for the warning signs of disordered eating
Advise the client to cut out the bedtime snack to keep the overnight glucose lower
Teach the client to have alcoholic drinks on an empty stomach to limit the calories
Advise the client to stop taking insulin to avoid further low blood sugars
questions.rat: Consistent meal timing prevents hypoglycemia, a registered dietitian referral is essential for an individualized meal plan, and the warning signs of disordered eating (omitting insulin, excessive exercise, unexplained A1C rise) warrant a mental health referral. Cutting out the bedtime snack and drinking alcohol on an empty stomach both promote hypoglycemia (alcohol should be taken with food), and stopping insulin would precipitate hyperglycemia and DKA — all three are unsafe.
questions.strat: Prevent lows with steady meals, the bedtime snack, and food-with-alcohol; refer to the RD and mental health — never stop the insulin.

questions.stem:
CASE (6/6) Evaluate outcomes. The client returns for follow-up after the plan was put in place several weeks ago.

Which finding best indicates the plan to manage the client's hypoglycemia and self-management is working?
opts: The client eats on a regular schedule with no further between-meal shakiness or sweating
The client reports eating the bedtime snack but is still skipping lunch on the busy workdays
The client has met with the dietitian one time and plans to schedule the mental health referral later
The client now carries glucose tablets and has needed them three separate times this past week
questions.rat: Regular meal timing with resolution of the between-meal hypoglycemia symptoms shows the underlying cause was corrected — the desired outcome. Still skipping lunch leaves the client at risk, completing only part of the referral plan is incomplete follow-through, and needing to rescue-treat lows three times in a week shows hypoglycemia is still occurring rather than being prevented.
questions.strat: "Working" means the root cause is fixed and symptoms stopped — not that rescue treatment is being used more.

questions.stem: An adult with type 1 diabetes plans to go for a 30-minute jog. Before starting, the capillary blood glucose is 268 mg/dL and the urine is positive for ketones. Which response by the nurse is most appropriate?
opts: "Have a 15-gram carbohydrate snack right now and then begin your jog."
"Hold off on the jog until the glucose drops and ketones clear."
"This is a good glucose level for exercise, so you may start right now."
"Wait about 30 to 60 minutes after eating, then go ahead and jog."
questions.rat: Exercise should be avoided when the blood glucose is greater than 250 mg/dL with ketones present, because activity can worsen hyperglycemia and ketosis. The snack answer fits hypoglycemia (glucose under 100), not this value; calling it a good level is incorrect; the timing-after-meals rule does not override the ketone red flag.
questions.strat: Glucose over 250 plus ketones = do not exercise; ketones change the whole answer.

questions.stem: The nurse reviews self-monitored glucose readings for a client with type 2 diabetes. Using the targets taught in the program, classify each reading as within range or out of range for the stated timing.
questions.rat: Premeal target is 70-130 mg/dL (118 within, 154 out), two-hour postprandial is under 180 mg/dL (168 within, 205 out), and bedtime is 90-150 mg/dL (132 within). Students often misjudge by applying the same cutoff to every timing instead of matching the value to when it was drawn.
questions.strat: Match each glucose value to its timing target: premeal 70-130, 2-hr postmeal under 180, bedtime 90-150.

questions.stem: The nurse is teaching foot care and injury prevention to a client newly diagnosed with diabetes. Which client statements indicate that teaching has been effective? Select all that apply.
opts: "I will look over my feet every day for cuts, redness, or sores."
"I should keep carbohydrate snacks with me when I exercise."
"If my glucose runs above 250 I will exercise harder to bring it down."
"I will keep eye exam appointments to protect my vision."
"Numbness or tingling in my feet is normal and I can ignore it."
"I will return-demonstrate checking my blood sugar at home."
questions.rat: Daily foot inspection, carrying carbohydrate during activity, routine eye exams for retinopathy, and demonstrating glucose monitoring all reflect effective self-management teaching. Exercising harder when glucose is over 250 is unsafe, and dismissing numbness or tingling misses a reportable neuropathy cue that raises injury risk.
questions.strat: On 'teaching effective' items, reject any statement that ignores a warning sign or adds risk.

questions.stem: A client with long-standing diabetes returns for a follow-up visit. While taking the history, the nurse gathers several findings. Which finding should the nurse address first?
opts: Reports new numbness and tingling in both feet
States that exercise has helped with weight loss this past month
Mentions a family history of high blood pressure and heart disease
Describes occasional dryness of the skin on both of the lower legs
questions.rat: New numbness and tingling signals peripheral neuropathy with loss of protective sensation, which raises the risk of unnoticed foot injury and infection and should be addressed first. The weight-loss progress is a positive outcome, family history is background data, and mild skin dryness is a lower-acuity cue that can be managed after the safety risk.
questions.strat: Prioritize the cue that creates the most immediate injury or safety risk, not background or positive findings.

questions.stem: A client with diabetes asks the nurse what nutrition and lifestyle changes might help with the neuropathy that has developed in the feet. Which response by the nurse best reflects the teaching in the plan of care?
opts: "Combining a plant-based diet with exercise may help your symptoms."
"Adding more red meat and full-fat dairy to your meals tends to help the nerves."
"Cutting nearly all carbohydrate out of your daily diet usually relieves it."
"Resting your feet and avoiding most physical activity is the best way to manage it."
questions.rat: The plan notes that a combination of a vegan (plant-based) diet and exercise may help treat neuropathy, so encouraging exercise and proper nutrition is appropriate. Loading meals with red meat and dairy is not the recommended approach, eliminating nearly all carbohydrate is not advised and risks hypoglycemia, and avoiding activity contradicts the recommendation to encourage exercise.
questions.strat: For diabetic neuropathy, the guide pairs a plant-based diet with exercise, not rest or extreme diets.
